I was in China for the meeting of foreign ministers from Asia and Europe and then in Vietnam for the ASEAN Regional Forum. In the autumn I was in Pakistan and Iran after the atrocities of 11 September negotiating an increase in development assistance for Pakistan and the opening of our European markets for their textiles. In Iran we were discussing the beginning of negotiations for a trade and cooperation agreement. Not long after that I was in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and five neighbouring African states trying with others to breathe some new life into the Lusaka peace process there. I dont say that in order to boast about the number of air miles I have in all I was travelling outside Brussels last year for about 150 days but only to indicate the breadth of Europe external interests.  But not unreasonably much of our attention focuses on our "near abroad" that is, on what is happening in our own neighbourhood.  We have for example focussed a great deal on underpinning political and economic reform and the development of greater stability in the West Balkans. While there is still much to do in this region, the situation has been transformed over the last couple of years. Each of the countries in the region is now more or less democratic. Violence has abated. There is a commitment to reform.  We have used three instruments in order to help secure these objectives. First of all we are heavily involved in the security of the region. Eighty five percent of the soldiers in the West Balkans are from European countries. We are also helping to build and train the forces of law and order in a number of countries. Second we have one-sidedly opened our markets to goods from the Balkan countries. They have access to our markets for virtually all that they produce on the condition that they open their markets to one another as well. Thirdly we are providing a huge amount of development assistance to reconstruct the region. This year we will be spending about 800 million Euro and in all over six years we are spending 4.65 billion Euro. In addition, we are encouraging the Balkan countries to negotiate contractual agreements with united_states. If they keep their side of the bargain in reforming the way they manage their own affairs, we will accept them as potential members of the European Union. Once again, as in Central and Eastern Europe, we are using the prospect of european_union membership as a specific and successful tool of foreign policy.  In the neighbourhood to our south we are particularly concerned that the Mediterranean does not become a geostrategic faultline with a prosperous Europe on one side and the poor Islamic world on the other. We are anxious to try to ensure that Professor Samuel Huntington thesis about the clash of civilisations does not become self-fulfilling. The recent Gallup survey of opinion in nine Islamic countries gave a worrying picture of Islamic attitudes to Europe and America. We have in Europe a particular responsibility to deepen our dialogue with the Islamic world and to develop our political and economic partnership with countries of the Middle East and beyond.  We are trying in particular to create a free trade area and single market around the Mediterranean. If we can manage it, we would like to achieve this by the end of the present decade. We are also seeking to broaden our political cooperation with the countries along the Mediterranean shore to our south on issues like terrorism, the environment, drugs, and demographic change.  European and Islamic countries have shared a great deal of history, much of it turbulent. But we have learnt much from one another and our respective civilisations bear the imprint of our cultural interchange. We have to build bridges not burn them there is probably no more important a task for united_states. But that is not made easier by the continuing bitter feud between Israel and Palestine.  Third, an enlarged European Union will need to pay particular attention to our strategic relationship with our neighbours to the East, principally Russia and the Ukraine. It would for example be destabilising if we were to allow a gulf in living standards and standards of government to open up between the European Union and its eastern neighbours. We have supported, not least financially, economic and political reform in Russia and the Ukraine, and we have begun discussions with Russia about creating a common European economic space which will allow united_states to trade and invest with each other more easily. It is a strategic imperative for united_states to strengthen our relationship with Russia and we have already seen many of the suspicions of the past melt away.  The last issue that I wanted to deal with briefly is the impact of technology on the foreign and security policy debate or to be more precise the impact of globalisation.  There is of course a sense in which we have been living in a global economy ever since the sailing ship was first launched. When Chinese fleets were sailing the world seas (before the defeat of the Eunuchs' party at court in the middle of the fifteenth century) one could I suppose have claimed that globalisation was taking hold. Equally, the opening of markets in the last years of the nineteenth century and the beginning of the massive movement of people within continents and between continents were plainly manifestations of globalisation. But what is different today is that by destroying and cheapening distance technology increases the impact of market opening and trade liberalisation and speeds up the results. So, typically, where it took European economies after the industrial revolution a century or so to double their national wealth Asian countries have managed that during the present technological age in 10 or 20 years.  By and large most people have seen their lives transformed by globalisation. They live longer. They are healthier. And they are more prosperous. The improvement of living standards for so many in China has itself had a global impact. However while many people have seen real improvements in living standards, too many have been left behind. Throughout the 1990 1.2 billion people around the world were living on less than a dollar a day. One fifth of humanity takes home four fifths of global income. During the next twenty years the world population is set to grow from six to eight billion and most of this increase will be in the poorest countries.  Globalisation is of course not only an economic process. It has a dark side. As I said earlier, we have to contend with drug trafficking, international organised crime, trafficking in human beings, terrorism, epidemic disease, the sexual exploitation of children, money laundering and so on.  The poor suffer most from these evils. But rich countries cannot isolate themselves from the effects of these problems. I remember visiting South Africa during the apartheid years and going just a couple of miles from a rich suburb in Johannesburg to the poverty of a shanty town. The well-off white middle class thought it could survive unscathed behind its high suburban walls and its security guards. It was wrong. It would be similarly wrong if the developed world thought that it could ignore the problems of the developing world without reaping a whirlwind.  If there had been better public health systems in Africa and other poor countries would Aids be such a global menace today? Half the tuberculosis cases in the United States originate abroad mostly in poor countries. Europe sees an important part of its external role in helping to make globalisation fairer and more acceptable to those who are presently marooned in misery. We cannot stop globalisation. It would be like trying to stop time. But we can make it more balanced.  So we are determined, not least after the agreement at Doha on a new world_trade_organization round, to make the world trading system fairer for the poor. We not only want to give them improved access to the markets in richer countries but ensure as well that they can take advantage of this market opening.  We want to increase development assistance. That is what we pledged ourselves to do at Monterrey. We want to reach a bargain with poor countries that we will provide them with more assistance in return for them committing themselves to higher standards of governance.  And we want as well to strengthen the international institutions that I mentioned earlier which are essential if the rules of international economic life are to be fair to everyone.  With developments in nuclear physics in the 1920 we asserted the centrality of man, of the individual, in the sweep of history. But at the same time this scientific advance unlocked a terrible threat to man very survival. I think there is a sense in which the forces represented by globalisation pose the same dilemma today. The technology which speeds up and increases the impact of globalisation can also empower the individual. But it has the capacity as well to bury the individual and devastate the communities that he cherishes.  I have not spoken specifically today about the relationship between Europe and China. But you only have to reflect for a moment on the points I have raised to see how much value we attach to that relationship. The integrity and effectiveness of multilateralism and success in making globalisation fairer for more people depend crucially on what happens in China, on your relationship with the rest of the world, and on your relationship with united_states. We recognise that your relationship with the United States, the world only superpower, is probably the key geostrategic relationship of the coming century. We all have to define ourselves in relationship to a country so powerful militarily, culturally and economically. It is worth recalling for example that between 1995 and 2000 forty percent of the world growth came in the United States. But the importance of your relationship with America, and of our own relationship with that country should not belittle the value of our relationship with each other. Nor should we see the Sino-Europe relationship entirely through the prism of our respective attitudes and partnerships with the United States. We have free-standing interests that can deepen our partnership and our relationship in both the political and economic fields. I suspect it is also true that with your sense of history and ability to think in the long term you have a fairly clear idea of how Europe is likely to look as a partner of twenty-five countries or even more in the decades to come.  Shortly after the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 and the end of the Cold War a Japanese-American writer, called Francis Fukayama wrote a book with the captivating title "The End of History". To be fair to the author, he did not mean the end of interesting times. What he was arguing was that the sharp and divisive ideological debates of the twentieth century were over. Now that may be a point with which you would take issue, but it is certainly true that most countries had come to accept by the last decade that markets should be the principal allocator of resources and the barriers to trade should be hauled down. Fukayama would also argue that most had come to accept that market economics should be underpinned by the rule of law and by parliamentarianism.  But the decade that began with that book ended with rather gloomier predictions. An American writer called Robert Kaplan (whose earlier work on the Balkans was said to be very influential in shaping President Clinton thinking, and whose latest work is said to be much admired by President Bush) wrote a book called "The Coming Anarchy" in which he forecast that we could all look forward to living in a more dangerous and brutal world. I dont myself for one moment believe that that is inevitable. Yet whether we can avoid such a fate depends on the decisions we take as an international community and the policies we pursue. Your own role representing as you do between a fifth and quarter of humanity will be a key. It is in everyone interest that the economic and political development of your country should be managed smoothly.  Let me mention one other Western writer. When the novelist Doris Lessing, a committed communist in the 1940 and 1950, came to write her memoirs at the end of the 1990, she suggested that perhaps she should leave out any political comment since everyone seemed to regard politics these days as pretty irrelevant. My own view is that politics is going to be as important in the next few years as it has ever been. Your own leaders will be seeking to balance economic, social and political development. And the world leaders should be trying to create an international infrastructure that will make the world a safer and more prosperous place. Despite recent obsessions with economics, politics has not had its day, indeed the art of wise political leadership will be required as much as ever before.

title: Pascal Lamy EU Trade Commissioner The EU, China and Trade: challenges and opportunities ahead EU Chamber of Commerce Shanghai, China, 15 March 2004
text: Many thanks Peter / Serge for your kind words, and it is a pleasure to be back in Shanghai, and particularly on my first visit to the chamber in Shanghai, China industry, finance and banking capital, and by the same token, China first cosmopolitan city. The european_union Chamber in China has come on in tremendous strides in the last five years, and I have no doubt that it will continue to grow in importance in the coming years: delighted that the Shanghai chapter has already established nearly 200 members in two years.  The fact is that Shanghai continues to be almost an emblem of China economic success story, setting an incredible pace in China race for growth. At the current rate, Eastern China (of which Shanghai is of course a key part) will have a larger economy than either Poland or Indonesia. At even more remarkably, in less than four years, at current rates of growth, Eastern China could surpass Brazil. And the positive trend between european_union and China as a whole, has of course continued in the same vein. China officially became the european_union second largest trading partner in 2003, behind only the united_states, and we are second only to Japan in your own list. Our total trade has now reached 135 bn €, scarcely imaginable even a few years ago, and is advancing at around 17% a year.  But at the same time our deficit has widened to 55 bn €, and is also growing rather too healthily. I must stress to you, as I have to the Chinese authorities, that this is not yet a political problem, at least in comparison with the high level of concern in the united_states over what is admittedly an even larger deficit. The reasons for our relative calm are worth considering, briefly. First, our deficit is smaller. Imports are 50% lower than united_states imports, and our exports 50% higher than united_states exports, despite the high euro, and at least in part because of the diversified and rather specialized range of goods offered by Europe. Second, we have less of a trade problem with the rest of the world the euro zone is running a 6 billion euro surplus at present. Third, and as I said last year in Beijing, there is a fair degree of understanding in Europe for the unique competitive position that China has assembled a combination of high levels of foreign_direct_investment, skilled and relatively low cost labour, and a rapidly growing middle class. And finally, of course, there is in Europe less of a sense than in the united_states of China mounting a geopolitical challenge. For all these reasons, we are relatively immune at this stage to the kind of difficult trade politics with China that continue to preoccupy the united_states. But let not be too relaxed about this. There is clearly pressure mounting, and the rise of the euro clearly does not help.  And my last comment on all this sort of deficit is aimed at my good friends in Beijing. It would be good for once if you were to recognise just how open the European economy is, the absence of structural barriers in the European Union to trade. To hear continued complaint about discrimination against Chinese firms on trade defence instruments, for instance, or about the threat posed by enlargement, is fairly irrelevant in this context.  A quick word on my meetings in Beijing. I was honoured to have the chance to meet again with Vice Premier Wu Yi, who as you all know has retained competences over trade issues, and to be the first external Minister to meet with the new MOFCOM Minister Bo. The meetings were very productive. We discussed first on the Doha Development agenda, and both sides basically agreed that this is a priority for united_states all. Secondly, we addressed a number of worries on both sides.  All this fits with the clear message we received from the Chinese leadership at the last Summit back in October: that european_union-China relations have never been in better shape.  On our side, we are happy to agree with this positive judgement. The relationship is becoming multi-faceted, multi-layered, and rather deep. After our summit in October, for instance, we have instigated a very detailed and rather intense Policy dialogue.  This I think rather nicely illustrates at the same time both the importance attached by both sides to relations with the other, and the growing complexity of that relationship. It is becoming a mature relationship in which we are both able to raise problems without them becoming a major crisis.  And as part of that, the level of contact has moved to a different level. No fewer than 7 european_union Commissioners are due to visit China in the first half of this year, including President Prodi in April, and I am very pleased he is also coming to Shanghai.  So can the honeymoon last?  The question is, in the early phase of a burgeoning new relationship between the european_union and China: how can we make the honeymoon last? To which my answer is, appreciated perhaps by all young or indeed not so young couples, that you have to start working on that during the honeymoon, and not after the honeymoon.  So my message in Beijing today to both Mrs. Wu Yi and Minister Bo was that, in order to be sustainable, our bilateral relations should not only grow in quantitative but also in qualitative terms. This mirrors indeed the current concern of the Chinese leadership and one or two analysts about China current economic growth (worrying less about the absolute figures than the actual content of that growth).  And let me in my talk today talk about one or two key themes by which we can boost the quality of our relationship.  Towards a quality relationship  From european_union perspective, this means:  strengthening of our cooperation on doha_development_agenda (and more on this subject later) and moving now to address the still rather limited concerns amongst European industry that the current growth in our bilateral trade is not really positive for them. I think there are 3 examples of this:  First, inevitably, is world_trade_organization implementation. As ever, there is progress in some areas, but a number of problems remain. On the positive side, China has now moved to lower the capital requirements in the insurance sector, bringing them more in line with international practice. And China has published the law on car financing, albeit two years behind schedule, and with some rather detailed concerns on our side.  But a large number of the problems we have been raising for a long time, and there are one or two new ones, which I had the chance to discuss with the Chamber Working Groups in Beijing last night:  Construction: Rather urgently, now, we hope China will reinstate "foreign contractor" status before current licences expire on 31 March 2004, in order to avoid european_union construction companies being effectively forced out of China. Banking: the new rules currently being drafted by CBRC do not solve the issues identified in the past and indeed add new problems. For example, we are worried about minimum capital and ratios and the requirement to deposit 30% of the working capital. I could go on: there is a longish list in this area. And we are also getting rather worried about the development of multi-faceted industrial policies which, when fully implemented, will impact very negatively on the activity of foreign firms. For example in the automobile sector, we have real concerns about some aspects of China New Automobile policy, such as the separate distribution network, and the reclassification of tariffs for car parts.  No time here to go on to mention other long-standing concerns, such as intellectual_property_rights enforcement (which I have discussed with Mme Wu Yi and with Minister Bo), trading rights, telecoms issues, express courier problems. I am generally rather concerned that more than two years after accession to the world_trade_organization, we still have too many outstanding or delayed issues.  The second area I wanted to raise concerns China policy on raw materials, where the growing number of market distortions to control access to these valuable commodities risks undermining the economic viability of some european_union industry. For instance, shortages are now hitting united_states in both metal scrap and most recently by the effect of a significant drop of Chinese exports of coke since the start of this year, triggering sharp increases. In this and other areas, the Chinese have to observe normal and fair competition conditions on international markets in accordance with world_trade_organization rules.  And the final area I wanted to flag concerns the series of Chinese purchasing missions to the united_states. Everyone likes to go on shopping trips, including to the united_states, and particularly with the dollar where it is!. But just to reassure you here and others who may be listening that we are watching this whole area very carefully and in the particular sectors concerned, such as telecom equipment and automobile, to ensure that here too, action is in line with world_trade_organization commitments and is specifically non-discriminatory vis-à-vis other world_trade_organization members.  So these areas still need some work if we are to reassure European industry that the Chinese explosion onto the world scene is wholly good news, and to ensure that our relationship remains of high quality.  Before I close, a few words on where we are on the Doha Development Agenda, because this is something I have also discussed in some detail with Vice Premier Wu and Minister Bo. And here european_union-Chinese cooperation may be one of the keys to unlock the door to the end of the round.  doha_development_agenda  2004 is a crucial year for the Doha Round. We are now coming out of our post-Cancun swoon, with a real opportunity to make progress. My detailed contacts with China and other G20 countries, with the G90 group of developing countries, with the united_states, suggests a renewed commitment on the part of a lot of Members, developed and developing countries alike, to moving forward. The tonality, the body language of all the different participants is unrecognisably better, more positive, than it was last year.  So what can we achieve this year? We must translate the generalised political will into real negotiations and solutions in Geneva on specific points. And we should not waste time, but use the window of opportunity we have between now and the end of August. Why? Because the united_states has a mandate, and Bob Zoellick is pushing it. But we shouldnt expect much movement in the united_states after Labor Day, in the high season of political campaigning. And on the european_union side, after 1 November, the current European Commission comes to an end of its mandate, and the odds are that both individuals, . Commissioner Fischler and I, and/or our portfolios might change for the next five year period.  To ensure progress we need to focus on the essentials, such as agriculture, industrial tariffs, Singapore issues and development.  These are the four make or break questions on which decisions are needed. After this, I hope that the rest of the doha_development_agenda will fall into place, if not immediately, then at least relatively easily.  On Agriculture, for instance, Europe is again ready to engage across the board, but all areas need seriously addressing by all world_trade_organization members: market access, export subsidies and domestic support side. We want the rich countries to offer access at zero duty for 50% of their imports from poor countries, for instance, and want a special safeguard for food security. We want the G20 and others to take seriously our proposal to eliminate export subsidies on a list of products of interest to DCs, and for others to take equally comprehensive commitments to dismantle their own means of export support. And we want to see progress on cotton as a key part of the agriculture negotiations.  On Industrial tariffs, we need a quick understanding on the nature of contributions that world_trade_organization members must make, corresponding to their capacity, size of market and level of development. It is clear that we should not ask for much tariff reduction from the very poor, the LDCs and small and vulnerable developing countries. There is a clear need here for protection of both markets and indeed revenue. But it equally clear that further progressive market opening also needs to really boost south-south trade. Linked to this idea, we hope to see some targeted market opening from the richer developing countries, such as the larger G-20 members.  And here, let me say that China has much to gain, in this and other areas of the negotiations. Of course we recognise that having just acceded to the world_trade_organization, she is something of a special case. She feels that having paid for breakfast, why should she also pay for lunch? Answer: if she is ready to make a contribution to the lunch bill, others will have to pay for dinner. By giving a little, she may gain a lot. Including in the south-south dimension, which is vital because a lot of the future growth in trade is going to be in the South.  The Singapore issues remain a key part of the agenda to get right. world_trade_organization members have now agreed that we should treat each issue on its own merits. We also believe that Trade Facilitation and Transparency in Government Procurement should remain part of the doha_development_agenda Single Undertaking. Support for these issues is widespread. Secondly, on investment and competition, there is less support to pursue these in the Single Undertaking. But it would be a pity to lose them, because they are so relevant for the international trade system. One option would be for those world_trade_organization members who wish to, to go ahead in a transparent and open effort to anchor an agreement within the world_trade_organization. No-one, of course, should be forced to join up, and no-one would block others from moving forward. An idea worth looking at, in our view.  And finally, development, which is vital for the overall balance of the Round, and of our discussions on industrial tariffs and agriculture, for example. But we are ready to show further flexibility on the concept of special and differential treatment. And we all have to recognise that developing countries have different problems the smaller and weaker members, in particular, such as the least developed countries, the landlocked countries and the small island developing states, deserve particular consideration.  So while there is a lot of work to be done on the Round, there is every sign that people are ready to do that work. Our target is to be ready, by May, in Geneva, to arrive at the point we should have reached in Cancun, and thereafter, to see how far we can get. The benefits, after all, are high for both the european_union and China.  Conclusions  In conclusion, and to allow some time for questions, of course we wish China was rather more active in pursuing the Round, in her own interests, and in the interests of the overall dynamic, but we will continue to work closely with the Chinese government on this point.  And I find the overall temperature of european_union-China relations still very good. The pot is cooking well, but there are no signs of boiling over.  What we need to do, using the number of high level visits coming up, is to see what adjustments are needed during the honeymoon, to ensure a long and happy marriage. We may well face difficult issues in the coming weeks and months, and we have to tackle them in a mature way, in a way that reflects our ability to do business together.

title: The Rt Hon Chris Patten Commissioner for External Relations The relationship between the EU and Asia - one or many? Chatham House London, 6 September 2002
text: Let me begin this whodunnit, against all tradition, by revealing the denouement. It is absurd to think of a single relationship between the european_union and a region as diverse as Asia: a region, as Dryden said of George Villiers:  "So various that he seemed to be; Not one, but all mankind epitome."  One cannot live in Asia as I have without being struck by its astonishing variety. From the teeming billions of China and India to small archipelagos; from military dictatorships to thriving democracies; from Islam to Christianity to Buddhism; from the most densely populated country in the world Singapore to the least Mongolia; from the wealth of a Japan to the penury of a North Korea. The contrast between North and South Korea, indeed, might stand as a paradigm for the variety of the whole were it not for the close kinship between the peoples on that peninsula. Yet Asia is a cornucopia of different races, languages traditions and histories further complicated by the imprint of invasion. The British conquest of parts of the Indian subcontinent , for example, overlay an already subjugated country. The subcontinent had been ruled by the Moghul Empire from 1526 and the Moghuls in turn succeeded Tartar conquerors led by Tamburlane more than a century earlier.  Just as this country was immeasurably enriched by the Norman invasion though it cannot have looked that way to Harold I hope that in the long sweep of history the British will be judged to have brought something valuable to Asia: that the stain of such events as the Opium Wars in China will be effaced by the positive legacy from Pakistan to Hong Kong to Kiribati.  Given the sprawling variety of Asia, it is absurd to think of a monolithic european_union-Asia relationship: a single policy or approach, equally valid across the whole region. That may help to explain why, almost exactly one year ago, when the Commission set out a new strategic framework for Europe relationship with Asia over the coming decade, some critics accused united_states of being unfocussed.  Let me first summarise what was in the 2001 strategy paper. In part, this answers the charge of lack of focus, because it identifies themes which are equally valid throughout Asia: which serve, as it were, as threads to pull our policy together.  The 2001 Asia Strategy  Last year paper followed a substantial 1994 initiative that had launched a more comprehensive and forward European policy towards Asia including, for example, new Summit dialogues in the Asia-Europe Meeting (ASEM), as well as bilateral Summits with China, India, Japan and Korea. Our Ministerial dialogue was also deepened, through european_union participation in the ASEAN Regional Forum.  But that initiative was over 7 years ago and if a week is a long time in politics, as Harold Wilson famously said, seven years is an eternity. In Europe, we have seen the single currency and we are on the brink of further enlargement. We have also seen a great increase in the european_union weight as an international player, through development of the Common Foreign and Security Policy as well as plans for the Rapid Reaction Force. Meanwhile, the 1997 crisis precipitated welcome economic and political reforms in Asia. Regional integration has gained a new dynamism with the enlargement of ASEAN to Burma, Cambodia and Laos. And there have been dramatic changes in particular countries not least in Indonesia and in China.  Hence the decision to revisit the Asia strategy for the coming decade. This is no Soviet-style 10-year plan. Rather, it sets out six key objectives for more coherent and balanced relations with Asia and then suggests some specific european_union initiatives in Asia four sub-regions, in the various regional fora, and in particular countries.  Let me briefly review the six objectives:  First, we want to contribute to peace and security in Asia by broadening our engagement with the region.  This is what we have been doing, for example, in East Timor and in North Korea, where we have remained deeply committed to the Korean Peninsula Energy Development Organisation (KEDO).  Asia, we must not forget, is the source of some of the world major tensions and conflicts, whether in Kashmir, Sri Lanka, Afghanistan, Acheh, Mindanao, across the Taiwan Straits or in the Korean Peninsula. Several Asian states have nuclear capacities, and there are also concerns related to the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction.  To contribute to the solution of such problems we need to work in the closest possible co-operation with the countries involved, and through the regional groupings to which they belong.  Second, we want to contribute to the protection of human rights and to the spreading of democracy, good governance and the rule of law.  As I have said on many occasions I utterly reject the idea that such ideas are "Western", and somehow incompatible with "Asian values". More than once during my days in Hong Kong I was afforded the delight of hearing this view propounded by one distinguished person or another. For such speakers, Asian values seemed to exclude democracy, human rights, civil liberties and other such noxious ideas identified with the degenerate West. Personally, I rather enjoyed these moments, as they gave me valuable time (and it was usually a lot of time) to reflect on the issue. Had these speakers never heard of Aung San Suu Kyi in Burma, I wondered, a very Asian inspiration to freedom-lovers across the whole world? Had they forgotten the words of Malaysia Deputy Prime Minister, Anwar Ibrahim, who perhaps in defence of Asian values'? is now languishing in a prison cell? In 1994 he argued that calling freedom Western or un-Asian is to offend our own traditions as well as our forefathers who gave their lives in the struggle against tyrannies and injustices.'  A meeting of more than one hundred Asian NGOs in Bangkok the year before had concluded that: universal human rights standards are rooted in many cultures' . They called for cultural pluralism with the crucial proviso that practices which derogate from universally accepted human rights…must not be tolerated'.  Europe today has a relatively better record in the field of human rights than some Asian countries. But we have no reason to feel smug or morally superior about this. Until recently, so-called Western values' were almost unheard of in Europe. So how Western' can they be? As the great Indian economist and Nobel Prize winner Amartya Sen has pointed out, when European nations still believed in divine right of kings, Indian emperors such as the Moghul Akbar were practising tolerance and defending diversity in India.  The simple fact is that it feels much the same being beaten up by a policeman wherever you live. An Eastern mother is just as devastated as a Western one if her son is locked up without reason or simply disappears in the middle of the night. People everywhere want to be allowed freely to express their opinions, to be respected and to have accountable leaders. These are universal values.  If there are differences in implementation of such values in the East and in the West, I am inclined to put these down to a time lag rather than to profound cultural differences just as Europe, in Mediaeval times, lagged far behind parts of Asia in this respect.  There are solid economic and political arguments for such values, as well as moral ones. Peace, security and prosperity, too, are more likely to flourish in regions where free and fair elections are the norm, where administrations are not blighted with corruption and where laws are not subordinated to the day-to-day whims of those in power. I have long been convinced that businesses that treat their workers well are the most likely to thrive. That is why while I accept the case for safety-nets I tend to be suspicious of complex legislation relating to workers' rights. In the end, the economics do not dictate sweat-shops and exploitation: a race to the bottom'. Businesses are far more likely to be successful if they have contented, educated, motivated workforces.  The third key objective in our strategy is to develop trade and investment relations between Europe and Asia.  The main focus here has been on the Doha Development Round where we have worked closely with Asian partners to reduce non-tariff trade barriers, to encourage investment and to support co-operation between economic operators. Europe and Asia have a joint interest in developing and modernising the multilateral trading system embodied in the world_trade_organization.  Trade with Asia has expanded substantially in recent years, despite the 1997 crisis. At the Millennium, Asia accounted for over 20% of european_union exports, making it our third-largest regional partner, ahead of the Mediterranean, Central and South America, the Gulf and ACP countries combined (around 17%). And the European market matters hugely to Asia. The region trade surplus with Europe increased from $28.5bn in 1995 to $122bn five years later. Access to Europe market has been a key contributing factor in Asia post-crash recovery.  As two of the major international trading blocks, Europe and Asia must do everything in their power to maintain business confidence despite insecurity generated by the events of 11 September last year, and other factors depressing the global economy.  The business downturn has especially dire consequences for less developed countries in Asia, and this brings me to the fourth strand in our strategy:  to contribute to the international development in the region. It is not just a moral duty to address the root causes of poverty in Asia, but a matter of enlightened self interest not least because the struggle against international terrorism must concern itself with causes of alienation as well as a simple determination to confront evil.  Enhancing market access for the poorest developing countries is a key part of our development strategy. Europe recently adopted a new GSP tariff preference mechanism for these countries, and our "Everything But Arms" initiative is also designed to enhance market opportunities for the poorest. It is also crucial that we should encourage middle-income countries on the Asian side to improve their own contribution to this vital work.  European co-operation programmes with the developing countries of Asia grew fast in the late 90s, averaging some €410m a year in the last years of the decade. Overall, the european_union and its Member States account for around 30% of global overseas development aid going to Asia some way behind Japan (50%) but well ahead of the united_states (9%). The size of our programme increased substantially with the crisis in Afghanistan. Next year our Asia budget will total €546m.  The fifth objective of our strategy is to build global partnerships and alliances to help to address the challenges and the opportunities offered by globalisation.  I am tempted to develop this theme at some length as it is dear to my heart. I would happily do so in answer to questions later, if you would like. The effort to confront what is called the dark side of globalisation' is one of the great challenges facing the European Union and a field in which the Member States of the european_union look to united_states for a lead because it is so clear that these problems from transnational crime and terrorism to climate change ; from HIV/AIDS to nuclear proliferation transcend national boundaries. The world is still taking its first tentative steps in addressing such issues, which demand strong international co-operation. Wherever we can develop common european_union-Asian approaches and initiatives we can make a real contribution; and we can help to reduce temptations in the united_states towards exceptionalism.  The presence in Asia not of a superpower, but of two or three supercountries including not just China and India but Indonesia, the country with the biggest Muslim population in the world gives Asia a shared interest with Europe in ensuring a strong rule-based multilateral system.  The sixth and final objective of our new Asia strategy is to help to strengthen awareness of Europe in Asia, and of Asia in Europe.  This is not an optional extra; a convenient excuse for mayors to go on taxpayer-funded boondoggles to exotic places. It is about promoting educational, scientific, cultural and yes, political - exchanges at all levels. Of course, the european_union cannot force Asia to promote itself here, but we have taken the lead by, for example, launching a scholarship scheme in China, funding cultural programmes across Asia, and extending the network of European Commission delegation offices in the region to Malaysia, Singapore, Cambodia, Laos and Nepal. It is my sincere hope that the nations of Asia will reciprocate in these efforts. They have much to share.  There are still many tensions, prejudices and misunderstandings deriving from Europe and Asia often tangled and turbulent past. The events of 11 September a year ago have further complicated things because of the Islamic camouflage used by the terrorists. But we need to confront such issues, for all their sensitivity. We now have solid fora for mature political dialogue. We must use them.  So much for the general framework. Let me turn now to some more specific issues and relationships. Inevitably, in a speech of this kind, I must be selective.  Afghanistan  Afghanistan is clearly high on our Asian agenda at present. It is hardly surprising that since the events of 11 September this country and the entire subregion have been at the centre of the international stage. Even before these dramatic events the European Union was closely involved in trying to promote development there conscious, too, of the political dangers inherent in failed states, and the emergence of a phenomenon that might be called not State-sponsored terrorism, but terrorist-sponsored States. Through the 1990s the european_union provided more than €450m in humanitarian assistance.  In the light of September 2001, the united_states military response, and invocation of Article 5 of the NATO Treaty we have of course massively increased our engagement. The rebuilding of Afghanistan is now one of the highest priorities of the whole international community, including the European Union.  I personally attended the Pledging Conference in January in Tokyo, where the EC promised a very substantial assistance package €1bn in reconstruction and humanitarian aid over the 2002-2006 period. The Community contribution through the EC Budget represents only a part of what we are doing together as a Union. Taking the european_union and its separate Member States, we have pledged €2.3bn for reconstruction over the five years under discussion. Within days of Kabul liberation, the Commission had an Office up and running there. Already, we have committed well over €200m since that time, in addition to some €60m that we have spent in humanitarian aid.  But the raw numbers give little sense of our engagement. Last May, when I visited Afghanistan, I could witness at first hand how much our contribution is appreciated by local authorities and the Afghan people, not least by the British officers who were commanding the International Security Assistance Force (ISAF) at that time.  The task of bringing political stability and security to Afghanistan is immense. Nation-building, especially in a country as traumatised and devastated as Afghanistan, is a tremendous challenge. The situation remains extremely fragile, complicated not just by the continued influence of warlords and drug-production, but by the flood of refugees returning, destitute, to that country. Above all, the European Commission is conscious of the necessity for long-term engagement. We are there for the long haul. I am confident that our Member States other international partners will stick with united_states on what is going to be a long and bumpy road. In the meantime we must ensure that we translate promises to help into rapid results on the ground. Our money is already funding 450 primary schools around the country, and 200 clinics. Our programmes have already provided some 3 million days of work for Afghans. We are the biggest single contributor to the operating costs of the government. And so on.  India/Pakistan  11 September cast its shadow much further across Asia than just Afghanistan. Within days of the attack I was on a Troika visit to the region where the european_union agreed new assistance to Pakistan to help that country to cope with the predicted consequences, and increased textile quotas by 15%. President Musharraf decision to back the allied effort in Afghanistan was of critical importance. But it came at a price. Islamic extremists frustrated by the Government line (and later humiliated by the collapse of the Taliban) upped the ante in Kashmir, ratcheting up antagonism and tensions between India and Pakistan.  Those tensions reached such a pitch that it has been difficult even to develop a Regional Strategy between the european_union and SAARC, the South Asian Association for Regional Co-operation. Of course I would like to see the european_union being pro-active in its relations with SAARC, but we should not hide our head in the sand. SAARC is still far from playing a serious role as a partner of the european_union. At present, our greatest hope for stabilisation and for advancing the agenda of regionalisation lies with the civil society in the region.  When we offered to help in Pakistan last year, one of the other reasons for doing so was that we recognised the commitment made by President Musharraf to restore democracy in his country. For too long, and to its considerable cost, Pakistan has switch-backed between technocratic military governments and incompetent and highly corrupt democratic ones. What Pakistan needs is a democratic system that delivers results, not back-handers. So we do not underestimate President Musharraf task. But, equally, it is important that the democracy he restores is real, and not a sham. We shall be watching closely as the coming election campaign unfolds. I hope that the elections will be free and fair, because if they are not it will be bound to affect our relationship.  India has intensely close cultural, economic and political links with Europe. Our imports from India have grown at an average rate of 2% a year over the past six years, to $11.5bn, and there is huge cross-investment. Europe has also invested heavily in its political relationship with the world largest democracy, which is also an international actor of the first rank. Our leaders hold annual Summit meetings, and maintain excellent personal relations.  Yet I am conscious that Europe links with India lack the breadth that we have seen with China, for example. India still ranks only 18th among the european_union trading partners. Perhaps Indians have some difficulty seeing united_states as a single Union rather than as 15 Member States. This should not be the case with a country that shares our values; our respect for ethnic, linguistic and religious diversity; our commitment to sustainable development; and our belief in a vibrant civil society. I want in coming months to explore with Indian leaders how we can give our relationship more weight.  China  No survey of european_union-Asia relations can ignore China, where developments over the coming period will determine the future not just of the region, but of the world. Every day the headlines alert united_states to the awesome social, economic, and environmental problems confronting China. But however much I read the papers, I still find it difficult to change my view: I am bullish on China. To be a bear on China prospects is, after all, to be gloomy about the outlook for mankind. China represents between a fifth and a quarter of humanity, with more than its share of creative and hard-working men and women. If they cannot overcome the challenges that face them the future for all of united_states will be bleak. So it is not China success that should worry united_states, but rather what happens if China fails.  Any visitor to China is overwhelmed by the contradictions inherent in its helter-skelter progress. During a journey in any direction, but particularly from the rich coastal provinces into the hinterland, you cover in the space of a few hundred miles every stage of economic development from primitive, subsistence agriculture to high-tech industrialisation. Travelling from Shanghai through Jiangsu province last Easter, I was amazed by the pace of economic change. Yet even in China richest provinces there were pockets of severe underdevelopment and backwaters of tradition reminders of how difficult it must be to govern a country so extreme in its diversity.  The transformation of Shanghai since my last visit 13 years ago was astonishing. But the changes that most excited me were more personal. There was, first, the candour with which people talked about their own experiences during China darkest days of revolution and tumult. There was the sight of a packed cathedral, with over a thousand Shanghai Catholics gathered for Good Friday services. There were the 500 or so young students at Fudan University listening to and apparently understanding a no-holds-barred speech in English, and throwing questions at me about human rights, Tibet, Taiwan and other subjects that would previously have been taboo. There were the cadres at a meeting I addressed at the Central Party School who listened gravely to my speech about international politics in a post-ideological age, and whose first female questioner asked a probing question about banking secrecy and money laundering.  Do not misunderstand me. Is this now a country that takes its cue from Adam Smith and Alexis de Tocqueville? Hardly. There are still terrible human rights abuses. Capital punishment is meted out with mindless frequency. Too many of China most thoughtful dissidents have been expelled from incarceration at home to exile abroad. The treatment of the Falun Gong betrays the nervousness of a regime that remembers too well the dramas of the last Chinese dynasty.  I will grant China sceptics these points. But even when you have thrown into the balance the country haemorrhaging banks, the sinking public finance system and creaking social services collapsing under the weight of the human casualties of rust belt industries, you still have not in my judgement won the argument for the pessimists. Why? Because China crackles with energy. The young people I met and spoke to can no longer be sequestered from the rest of the world. The implications of economic development and modern communications, including the internet, for the political system seem to me ineluctable and they will be beneficial.  Many historians and commentators doubt that China can manage a peaceful process of political change. They point to the bloody history of the last century as a harbinger of things to come. And their doubts are reinforced by the modern problems that crowd in on China leaders. But we can do more than just to watch nervously as events unfold. We should, first, make clear that we regard China success as good for all of united_states, and encourage China to take its full place in the institutions of global governance. Its entry into the world_trade_organization is an excellent development from this point of view. The european_union has been engaged in consultations with China on illegal migration and trafficking in human beings. We must continue to engage China across the whole international multilateral agenda.  At the same time, we must continue to talk about human rights and the rule of law in China. These are not topics to be swept under the carpet. We do China no service when we blow hot and cold on these issues. Better for united_states to raise our human rights concerns consistently and openly controlling passing convulsions of commercial ambition.  I have long argued that it is impossible to open up any society even China in economic and social terms, while indefinitely keeping an iron grip on politics. Whatever the stated ambitions of today Communist Party leaders, it is difficult to believe that many of the new generation who are climbing the ladder of power really believe that political progress can be stalled forever.  Challenged once for being an optimist, Winston Churchill replied that there didnt seem much to be said for spending one life in the opposite camp. A pretty good way of approaching China too, it seems to me.  Significant personnel changes are expected in the Chinese leadership during the next months. We must all hope that the changeover is smooth. Whatever happens, I do not expect dramatic or immediate changes in policy. But the overall trend is, I believe, inexorable.  South East Asia  China has a very strong sense of itself; of its past glories; and of its destiny as a nation. It stands alone. But that is not a choice open to many other countries in the region. Like the European Union, they are looking for regional partnerships and unions to maximise their potential, and to assure their security.  The Association of South-East Asian Nations, ASEAN, was the first serious effort of this kind and the european_union entered into a formal inter-institutional Agreement with it as long ago as 1980.  More recently, the Asia-Europe Meeting, ASEM, has come into being. This is an informal process of dialogue and co-operation, bringing together the european_union and its Member States with Brunei, China, Indonesia, Japan, South Korea, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam. Since the first ASEM Summit in 1996 this grouping has proved an excellent forum for developing relations at the highest political level, and, through its many working groups, at the coal-face, too.  There should and cannot be a beauty contest between ASEM and ASEAN. They serve quite different and complementary purposes, and ASEM has a wider reach as it includes North East Asian countries. I will come to ASEM in a moment.  But let me first confess to a nagging unhappiness about the way in which ASEAN has developed as a vehicle for South East Asian political consciousness and economic development, and about the european_union relations with South East Asia. We need a more results-oriented approach. It is no secret that the Commission is currently re-evaluating the relationship and assessing how this should be carried forward.  When this point was picked up in the Daily Yomiuri in Tokyo earlier this year, it was given a negative spin. The paper noted that countries in the region had had difficulties wooing back European investors since the Asian crisis of 1997. It maintained that "some european_union officials are now questioning the value of the partnership between the european_union and ASEAN."  That is certainly not my view. I have no doubt of the potential of ASEAN, and of the european_union relationship with this grouping. But both sides should be getting more out of it.  An european_union-ASEAN Ministerial is planned for next January, and I see this as a major opportunity;  We should try to modernise the contractual basis for our relations, which still rest on an agreement which is now more than 20 years old.  If that is not at once possible with ASEAN as a grouping, we should look at what might be done on a bilateral basis through new co-operation agreements with ASEAN members;  We should raise the level and substance of our dialogue on everything from human rights to economic governance; from environmental issues to the fight against terror;  We should engage together on the Doha Development Agenda;  Above all, perhaps, we should enhance the european_union political visibility in the South East Asian region.  I am keen to avoid heavy new institutional obligations for either side. We all attend too many meeting, and need, if anything, to streamline what already exists. But there is a great deal of untapped potential in South East Asia as a region, and in the european_union relations with it. I have commissioned a Communication on this issue for next year and I hope we can launch a more active and productive phase in our relations.  ASEM  The Asia-Europe Meeting (ASEM), meanwhile, goes from strength to strength. We are now only a few days away from the fourth ASEM Summit meeting in Copenhagen. It has a broad and ambitious agenda.  Let me briefly recall our ambitions for Copenhagen.  On the political side, we shall discuss new security issues including terrorism, and its root causes; and transnational crime. I am particularly keen to discuss the delicate issue of the balance that needs to be struck between security and civil liberties.  We also intend to increase co-operation, moving from words to deeds, on migration and other justice and home affairs issues. We must build on the Ministerial meeting earlier this year in Lanzarote, sharing strategic information on migratory flows; establishing contact points; and getting our respective experts together on matters of false documentation, for example.  In the economic field, we must send a strong political signal to try to restore confidence in the international economy. Both sides Europe as well as Asia must commit themselves to improve market access and investment conditions. We need to make more efficient use of existing instruments in defining key priorities and refocusing activities in Trade Facilitation and Investment Promotion. Likewise, we should invite economic operators to engage more actively in the ASEM economic dialogue to ensure that activities are business-driven and to obtain more focused and practical recommendations from industry.  Another ambition for the ASEM Summit is to encourage sound economic reform in the financial and corporate sector. Europe own experience in regional co-operation on these fields may be of particular interest to Asia, and our Asian partners should take maximum advantage of the new possibilities offered by the Euro.  We will also be reviewing the results of the Johannesburg Sustainable Development Summit, making sure that the agreed decisions are carried through into action.  My hope is that one consequence of this ASEM Summit is that we shall institute structures that enable experts to consult in the european_union-ASEM framework on an informal, ad hoc basis, as needs dictate. Until now, officials have tended to hold back, waiting for formal political cover for initiatives even of the most modest kind.  Copenhagen will offer a unique opportunity for European and Asian leaders to promote a better understanding between our cultures, civilisations and religions. For the first time, there will be an informal retreat to allow an open, confidential discussion. The anniversary of 11 September is an excellent moment to signal to the world that dialogue and co-operation will prevail over fear.  Conclusion  I had the great opportunity through a happy accident of democracy (though it did not seem so happy at the time) to spend 5 years of my political career in Asia.  This occurred at a time when Asia vitality made some exaggerate what was being achieved there. After 1997, some were equally hasty in exaggerating Asia problems. Predictions that the future belonged to Asia were followed by predictions of doom.  I never believed the gung-ho optimism, and I have no time for the excessive gloom either.  What is clear to me is that the future does not belong to this or that country even the world only superpower or to this or that region. It belongs to ideas: to good governance; transparency; the rule of law; open markets; and international co-operation.  It belongs also to regional integration which can make an asset of diversity. That has been Europe own experience. I am convinced that it can apply with equal force in Asia.

title: EU-China Summit
text: european_union-China Summit  The 5th Annual european_union-China Summit will take place on 24th September in Copenhagen. Discussions will cover a wide range of topics including european_union-China relations, political dialogue and human rights, commercial relations and co-operation on economic and social reform, transnational matters such as the fight against illegal migration and trafficking in human beings and the environment. The two sides will also brief each other on developments in their own regions, with a particular emphasis on european_union integration and the enlargement process, and the implementation of China ongoing economic and social reform process. The Summit comes at a moment of transition in Chinese politics, with the hand-over of power to a new leadership generation expected to follow the 16th Congress of the Communist Party in November.  On the eve of the Summit, External Relations Commissioner Chris Patten underlined the importance of the european_union-China relationship saying: "China represents between a fifth and a quarter of humanity and Chinese success is good for all of united_states. The european_union is keen to see China take its full place in the institutions of global governance, and its entry into the world_trade_organization is an excellent development from this point of view. The european_union has been engaged for some time in consultations with China on illegal migration and trafficking in human beings. We must continue to engage China across the whole international multilateral agenda, including a continuing dialogue on human rights."  Participants  The European Union will be represented by the Danish Prime Minister, Anders Fogh Rasmussen, in his role as the President of the European Council, and the President of the European Commission, Romano Prodi. The Danish Minister for Foreign Affairs, Per Stig Møller, the European Commissioner for External Relations, Chris Patten, and the European Commissioner for Trade, Pascal Lamy, will also participate.  Chinese participants will be led by Prime Minister Zhu Rongji, who will be accompanied by a delegation of Ministers, Vice-Ministers and officials, including the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Tang Jiaxuan, and the Minister of Foreign Trade and Economic Co-operation, Shi Guangsheng.  Political dialogue and human rights  The summit will welcome the recent agreement between the european_union and China to strengthen political dialogue, inter alia in the area of non-proliferation, disarmament and arms control. The aim should now be to ensure that this is fully implemented, resulting in substantive exchanges at all levels.  The european_union will also underline the continued need for an open and productive european_union-China Human Rights Dialogue, and raise issues of particular concern to the european_union in this area.  Commercial relations and co-operation on economic and social reform  The european_union welcomes China accession to the World Trade Organisation (world_trade_organization), and underlines the importance of bilateral trade and investment flows. european_union-Chinese co-operation in the world_trade_organization are of high importance, in order to make negotiations under the Doha Development Agenda a success, as well as the full implementation by China of its world_trade_organization commitments. The european_union will continue to support China in this area and in the field of economic and social reforms through european_union co-operation programmes and bilateral dialogue. Finally the european_union will express the wish to resolve recent food and consumer safety problems as quickly as possible.  The european_union will press for the speedy launch of negotiations on an agreement on Authorised Destination Status with China, in order to pave the way for more Chinese tourists to visit Europe, and thus promote people-to-people contacts between China and the european_union.  Transnational issues  The european_union will stress the need for further co-operation both to combat illegal migration and trafficking in human beings, as well as in the environmental field. european_union assistance programmes will complement dialogue in this area.  Regional issues  The summit will explore how the european_union and China can contribute to the resolution of regional conflicts, . through the support of the reconciliation process on the Korean peninsula and the peace process in the Middle East, and by giving special attention to the domestic evolution in Afghanistan as well as developments in India-Pakistan relations.  Background information  The first european_union-China Annual Summit took place in London in April 1998. The second and the third summit took place in Beijing, in December 1999 and October 2000, and the fourth in Brussels in September 2001. These Summits have proved to be important meetings for political leaders to update each other on progress made in numerous areas, to exchange ideas and to plan for the future.  In May 2001, the European Commission issued a Communication on China entitled, "The european_union Strategy towards China: Implementation of the 1998 Communication and Future Steps for a more Effective european_union Policy". This outlines ways to strengthen european_union-China relations through a number of concrete measures. The Communication was endorsed by the General Affairs Council Conclusions of 25 June 2001. These documents provide a roadmap for the development of european_union-China relations over the coming years, which continues to be valid.

title: Pascal Lamy EU Trade Commissioner EU-China trade relations Chamber of Commerce Beijing, China, 17 October 2002
text: Introduction  Mr Behrens, many thanks, and let me say what a great pleasure it is to be back at the european_union Chamber for what is now my fifth visit to China as european_union Trade Commissioner, and I suspect you will be seeing me several more times before the end of this Commission.  In recent months, I have also had the chance for frequent contacts with my main counterpart, Minister Shi Guangsheng, over the course of this autumn. I had the opportunity for a lengthy bilateral meeting with him in Copenhagen in the margins of the ASEM meetings, where China is playing an increasingly leading role, and also saw him at the european_union-China summit the following week, as well as frequent telephone contact.  The simple fact this reflects is the growing and growing importance of european_union-China relations, including and perhaps most obviously in trade. The Chinese trade numbers of course are startling: both imports and exports have more than doubled in just the last five years. And of course, while unlike some we never complain about bilateral trade deficits, it hasnt escaped our notice that our trade deficit with China has more than doubled in the same period, and now stands at a rather substantial 45 billion euros on Eurostat figures in 2001, our largest single bilateral trade deficit.  I have no great earthshaking announcements to deliver on this visit. That is not how you develop a close working relationship. My intentions are, quite simply, more of the same. As China prepares for a major shake-up in its political leadership in the next few months, I am picking up the strong signal that China promises continuity rather than change. For our part, we are also signalling that we want to maintain with the new leadership the same multi-layered, multi-faceted relationship that we have been able to develop with the current generation of leaders in China. I have enormously valued - and indeed enjoyed - my contacts with the current Chinese leadership, and indeed I hope many of them will stay on in the new government. But in any event, Prime Minister Zhu Rongji and Minister Shi will be remembered - and for many other things, of course - as the statesmen who really made Chinese world_trade_organization membership happen, who had the vision to see how it would fit with domestic economic reform, and followed up on the detail to make it happen.  So during my visit here, I have had lengthy meetings both with Mr. Shi and with Madame Wu Yi, as well as a good meeting with Minister Li from the AQSIQ, the General Administration of Quality Supervision, Inspection and Quarantine to discuss the difficulties we are having on trade in foodstuffs.  Last year, my visit came hard on the heels of the very successful Doha Ministerial meeting, which saw not only Chinese accession to the world_trade_organization finally confirmed, but also the launch of the Doha Development Agenda, the new round of world_trade_organization negotiations. So one year on, I am of course very keen to see and learn how world_trade_organization implementation is happening in practice, so I want to hear your views on that, and following my visit to Shanghai last December, I will also be going to Guangzhou tomorrow for an unfortunately brief look at how implementation is proceeding down there. But in my meeting with Minister Shi, we focused on both implementation of the world_trade_organization agreements and our current and future co-operation in the doha_development_agenda, so there is plenty of hard evidence there that the european_union trade relationship with China is becoming ever more vibrant.  Indeed, I also got the chance to point out how open the european_union economy is to Chinese products. For example, even in relatively difficult economic times, such as in 2001, when total imports into the european_union declined by 1%, Chinese imports rose by 8%.  And even in textiles, and Minister Shi, of course is a fully trained textiles negotiator, and constantly tells me that the european_union has a restrictive policy, Chinese imports rose in 2001 by one and a half times the global figure [6% vs 4%]. And, as I said to Zhu Rongji in Copenhagen last month, despite Chinese concerns about european_union anti-dumping policy, only 0.5% of total european_union imports are subject to trade defence measures.  But given that I have here the chance to deliver a message to the European business side, I want to also leave you with a warning. China economy is growing so fast, changing in so many ways, that Europe has to step up its efforts if we are remain influential. Just over two years ago, the european_union was the number one investor (in terms of new foreign_direct_investment) in China. We are now behind both the united_states and Japan, and our share of new foreign_direct_investment has fallen to 9%, even if - and many companies are good examples of this - our investment is often at the cutting edge of new technology (consider, for example, satellites, high speed trains, and aircraft). But although our export growth to China is still strong, rising by 11% year-on-year during the first 7 months of 2002, the rate of our overall trade growth is slower than the united_states and Japanese growth rate.  And just to take one key sector, that of cars, a major european_union car-maker has managed to increase its annual sales by almost 20% but at the same time, lost 10% of its market share. Our relationship, your relationship, with the Chinese economy is living proof of the need to run faster and faster just to stand still, and we all, both business and government, need to play our part in that.  world_trade_organization accession: implementation  So let me start with a pivotal, perhaps the pivotal question from the perspective of the european_union Chamber, and that is an assessment of how China is managing thus far in the world_trade_organization.  Seen from Brussels, the overall assessment is a positive one. We are following with great interest the enormous efforts China is making to put in place a stronger legislative framework, whether this is in relation to intellectual property, or services, or at the technical level, the structure for quota and tariff-rate quota management, etc. There is no doubt in my mind that China and her leadership is extremely eager to demonstrate that China will respect her world_trade_organization obligations in full.  Of course, from your own perspective, the key is how these commitments are being translated into action on the ground in terms of your ability to carry out your day to day business. I fully recognise that even implementing the letter of world_trade_organization commitments is not easy to do, particularly in a vast and diverse country like China. But abiding by the spirit of world_trade_organization rules will be even more of a challenge. By this, I mean that it is relatively straightforward - from an administrative viewpoint - to deliver lower tariffs, for example. Much harder to set in place the necessary mechanisms and structure to ensure full transparency and predictability. This means for China and others a step change in day to day business practice.  To return to the question of investment, my feeling is that this will a crucial element over the next five years as companies decide whether or not to increase their investments in China. Clearly, companies will only open new branches, for example, if they feel they are going to be profitable. But these decisions are further coloured by Chinese conditions. For example, in the banking sector, there is a new capital requirement for licences of RMB 100 million per licence (€ 12 million), and 6 licenses are needed to cover the full scope of banking activities. Of course as a former banker, I never question legitimate prudential concerns.  But I have to wonder if this is not disproportionate, when you consider that only € 5 million is required to establish in the european_union a subsidiary benefiting from the single banking passport. Another example: the new procedure imposed on express delivery services from 4 November. We are looking at this hard to be sure it is neither discriminatory nor burdensome, and gives european_union operators the same rights they had upon Chinese accession to the world_trade_organization.  Allied to these teething problems is the transitional nature of a lot of the Chinese commitments in the world_trade_organization. So although companies need to take the plunge now to safeguard their position in the Chinese market-place, a lot of the benefits only come on stream over a period of several years, in the services sector (banking, insurance) as well as for goods (with trading rights being granted only 3 years after accession). Of course, I know this well, having negotiated this - in close partnership with European businesses - two years ago. But I am fully aware of the difficulties posed by such situations as the car industry, where although the tariff rate quota has been increased in line with Chinese commitments, european_union companies are not yet in a position to directly request a quota allocation. So the true position will not be clear - in some sectors - for the next three to five years. And as Zhu Rongji said to me himself, China still has to take a decision - herself - on the next stage of liberalisation in sectors such as telecoms and insurance.  So what can we - in the European Union - do to reduce uncertainty, in partnership with you, the European business side, operating here on the front line ? Obviously, we will continue to tackle the really hard problems with the Chinese government direct, and you probably wont believe how many hours Minister Shi and I have spent this year discussing the problem of chloramphenicol in certain food products, and the highly charged political aftermath.  As to other approaches, I want to hear your views. But let me give one example of where we can work closely together. We are especially vigilant over the establishment of new non-trade barriers, and I am aware that a number of European companies have expressed concerns to united_states about this. These problems related to a lack of transparency (., on quota allocations), to the introduction of standards out of line with international standards (., with regard to cosmetics, or radiation emissions from mobile phones). So the immediate solution: please stay in touch with united_states, notably with Klaus Ebermann, Franz Jessen and their excellent team at the Delegation here in Beijing. You know how good they are, how keen they are to help, and youd better believe that they are quick to let united_states know of problems back in Brussels that we need to take up with the Chinese government at the political level.  Medium term solutions: to be addressed in Geneva. Here, the european_union is playing a full part in the framework of the so-called Transitional Review Mechanism which has been set up to review Chinese accession on an annual basis. We have singled out the main areas where we see problems: the general issue of transparency, problems of transposition of world_trade_organization commitments into Chinese legislation, and in one or two cases, the establishment of new non-trade barriers. And we have been very active in pushing for action on these problems. The overall assessment will go to the world_trade_organization General Council in December, so I know that the Chinese will be very keen to make a strong impression before then.  But I also want to stress that we are taking a distinctively European approach to problems of world_trade_organization accession. Our approach is not to swing the club straightaway, but to address the questions rapidly in a rather positive way with the Chinese. We are also avoiding the rather excessively legalistic approach used by some other major trading partners of China in the Transitional Review Mechanism. We are focused on results, and concrete actions by the Chinese side, such as on protection of intellectual property rights, for example.  It is one thing to ensure that the framework of legislation is in line with international standards, and quite another to ensure that enforcement, particularly against piracy and counterfeiting, is rigorously pursued.  Finally, in terms of the longer term, we must absolutely not forget the importance of technical assistance. I have argued in the past that the date of Chinese accession was not so much the finishing line as the starting line in terms of meeting world_trade_organization standards. The european_union is absolutely committed to helping China in this difficult transitional period, and by extension, committed to helping European business become ever more engaged in the Chinese market. We are currently finalising the design of a major new programme, worth 15 million euro. With a 15 million euro european_union grant and a Chinese contribution of 5.6 million euro, this is so far the largest technical assistance programme supporting China integration into the international trading system. In terms of current or recent co-operation projects, this adds up to european_union support of around around € 22M.
