Combining financial assistance with firmness where necessary is very much the European approach. Carrot and stick are both needed: the use of stick alone just causes political tension.  And - final word on this - what does this mean in terms of the final stage, of going to world_trade_organization dispute settlement proceedings if we do not manage to solve a particular dispute ? Some have speculated that we will not want to "upset" China by going to dispute settlement resolution. Well this is untrue. I am absolutely ready to go to the world_trade_organization where other courses of action have produced no effective results. But we dont, I think, help matters by dragging China into the world_trade_organization at the first sign of a problem. Our preferred approach is to recognise the size of the challenge, and to try to build confidence in the systems, both in the european_union and China, to facilitate trade. Of course I am ready to go to the world_trade_organization if necessary, but only if other methods fail.  The Doha Development Agenda  Before I give you the floor to hear your views on current problems, I would like to say a word about the importance of the growing collaboration on the doha_development_agenda between the european_union and China. Sometimes it may seem odd to you, as businessmen, that the european_union and China, after 15 years' long march into the world_trade_organization, are plunging again into fresh negotiations to further liberalise market access on an most_favored_nation basis, and to again improve the rules which govern world trade.  But this project is of critical global importance if we are to keep the world_trade_organization relevant, and I have found the Chinese remarkably open to European ideas in the doha_development_agenda, and I hope vice versa. We have, in Geneva, and between capitals, started to develop a very close working relationship on all the key issues, and I think we have found many common interests. We are impressed by the quality of Chinese contributions to the debates in Geneva, by the level of commitment to the doha_development_agenda not just in the world_trade_organization but also in regional forums such as the ASEM and APEC, and by the practical and businesslike solutions which China is putting forward, for the most part.  Of course, there are one or two exceptions. Perhaps understandably, China does not have major plans to open its own market further in terms of classical market access, and will inevitably mount the argument that she should not have to pay twice. That may be fair, but the fact is that in trade rounds, you have to be ready to pay to play, and I think China recognises this for example in the area of services where she has come forward with a surprisingly ambitious and comprehensive request list.  Either way, my message to you, the European business community, is that we are intensifying our work with the Chinese government, and so we are very keen to hear your priorities so we can put together an ambitious result in the Round. Clearly, we face some very difficult times ahead in the world economy, which will be sharply felt in both Europe and China. Difficult times which will, moreover, be made worse if war erupts in the Middle East. So it is in both China and the european_union interest to have an ambitious outcome in the Doha Development Agenda, and so I am doubly pleased at the strong commitment made by the Chinese leadership to this effect, both in the european_union-China summit statement, and in the words of Minister Shi himself.  As to the detail, we are now assembling a major bilateral dialogue with China on the new Round, and a team of world_trade_organization experts will be flying out to Beijing next month to launch that process. We are particularly keen to work on a number of areas of interest, such as trade defence instruments and safeguards (where I think we can both agree that the rules need updating), technical barriers to trade, and agriculture. We also see good prospects for working together on the so-called new issues such as investment, public procurement, trade facilitation, and competition. These are of course vital issues for the european_union and european_union business, but they are also development issues: rule-making issues are a key to continuing to attract good quality investment, and improving the quality of public administration. Progress in these areas means more stability and predictability for companies, lower costs of trade transactions, and better export performance. In terms of the current needs of the ailing world economy, it is not surprising that we are seeing the importance of the Doha Development Round rising up the political agenda.  Conclusion  Last year saw the launch of a new era for China and the world_trade_organization, a new era which sees China emerging rapidly as one of the key poles of the world economy. This year, we got down to business. The message I take from this year is that China is absolutely committed to delivering on the terms of her world_trade_organization accession package, and that the reward / effort ratio in Beijing remains very high. But in addition, close, close cooperation and collaboration: between the european_union and the Chinese authorities, and between ourselves, is going to be increasingly vital in terms of addressing the specific trade irritants we face, in terms of moving our trade relationship forward.  The fact is that - together - the european_union and China can make an enormous contribution to keeping markets open, to keeping the world_trade_organization steaming ahead, and to strengthening global economic governance.  I am very pleased, therefore, that all the signs are that China remains very much on track both economically and politically to claiming her rightful place at the global economic top table.  Thank you very much: hope we still have time for your questions and comments.

title: Pascal Lamy EU Trade Commissioner The importance of EU-China commercial relations Zhongshan University Guangzhou, China, 18 October 2002
text: Introduction  I am delighted to be here in Zhongshan University to say a few words about the importance of european_union-China commercial relations.  The story of Guangdong province  And there can be no better place to talk about european_union-China trade relations than the famous Guangdong province, the very byword for Chinese economic power and potential.  Guangdong of course was the home of China first modern revolutionary organisation, Sun Yat-Sen United League, back in the 1920s. And Sun himself founded Zhongshan University. Guangdong has now become synonymous with another modern revolution in China, the extraordinary economic reforms that have taken root since Deng Open Door Policy of 1978. Of course, as real estate dealers like to say when selling houses, the key question is "location, location, location", and Guangzhou in particular has a history of using its location on the Pearl River delta to great effect.  But Guangdong province always had more than just proximity to Hong Kong: it had a tradition of contacts with the outside world on which to draw; and an innate commercial flair. When Deng came to South China in 1992, and said boldly that Guangdong should become Asia "fifth tiger", his statement unleashed the tide of inward investment and economic growth to the point that - as you know - Governor Lu has recently and rather confidently - predicted that Guangdong would surpass the other four tigers within the next few years. Today, the Pearl River delta pulls in one quarter of China foreign direct investment and is responsible for an astonishing one third of China total exports. And as reported in the Far Eastern Economic Review, Guangdong Galanz Enterprise, now accounts for 40% microwave ovens sold in Europe.  I tell you this as a demonstration of what I was taught, some years ago, in university: always begin a speech by telling the audience something it already knows, and agrees with. I know I am preaching to the converted when I say how impressive your economic performance has been for the last twenty years. As the Economist newspaper put it only last week, the story is of "the emergence of an industrial region so huge as to transform global trading patterns and investment flows".  But as the people of this region also understand very well, with great successes come great challenges. If Guangdong is a microcosm of modern globalisation, it is not surprising that there is a downside of economic success that has to be managed as well as an upside to be enjoyed.  First, how do you ensure that your public services keep pace with your economic performance ? As Governor Lu said with great candour, even in Guangdong, there are still nearly a million families too poor to send their children to school. Second, how do you ensure that the local environment is not dramatically affected by economic growth? Talking to people today in this area, it is clear to me that Guangdong itself is the first to admit that the rapidity of economic change has posed great environmental challenges in areas such as air and water pollution. And thirdly, when you have set such a pattern, such a rhythm, of economic growth for such a sustained period, how do you cope when export growth, indeed growth itself, starts to flag as happened at the end of the last decade?  It was hardly surprising in the context of the rest of the Asian crisis of 1998, and the slowdown was less severe than in many other places, but it demonstrated that nowhere on this planet is safe from recession.  In two words, and it is clear to me that this is what Guangdong province is reaching for, how do you ensure sustainable development ? That is the challenge we face globally, and it is very topical to be able to address this just a couple of months after the Johannesburg World Summit on Sustainable Development. The thesis I want to put to you today is twofold: firstly, that one of the absolute keys to sustainable development is developing tools of global governance; and secondly, that european_union and China are emerging as two of the key poles of the global governance system, and so our co-operation becomes even more vital.  To illustrate this general point, I would like to focus in detail on two aspects in particular: first, how much China and the european_union are already working together in the new trade round in the World Trade Organisation, the Doha Development Agenda. And secondly, the continuing importance of implementing Chinese world_trade_organization accession commitments. These two aspects were also the two key points I discussed with Chinese Ministers in Beijing yesterday, and these two points - I was not surprised to see came up again and again during my all too brief tour of Guangdong.  But perhaps the business school students that are present here will forgive me if I begin with a short discussion of the growing importance of european_union-China trade relations. The Chinese trade numbers - of course often driven from the strength of the local economy here in Guangzhou - are startling: both your imports and exports have more than doubled in just the last five years. And of course, while unlike some we never complain about bilateral trade deficits, it hasnt escaped our notice that the european_union trade deficit with China has more than doubled in the same period, and now stands at a rather substantial 45 billion euros on Eurostat figures in 2001, our largest single bilateral trade deficit.  Indeed, I also had the chance when in Beijing to point out to Chinese Ministers, such as my counterpart, Shi Guangsheng, how open the european_union economy is to Chinese products. For example, even in relatively difficult economic times, such as in 2001, when total imports into the european_union declined by 1%, Chinese imports rose by 8%. And even in textiles, and Minister Shi, of course is a fully trained textiles negotiator, and constantly tells me that the european_union has a restrictive policy, Chinese imports rose in 2001 by one and a half times the global figure [6% vs 4%]. As I said to Zhu Rongji in Copenhagen last month, despite Chinese concerns about european_union anti-dumping policy, only 0.5% of total european_union imports are subject to trade defence measures, such as anti-dumping. So we effectively have our own version of the "Open Door" policy, launched by Deng back in 1978. The european_union really is open to trade with China.  Last year, I came to China just after the very successful Doha Ministerial meeting, which saw not only Chinese accession to the world_trade_organization finally confirmed, but also of course the launch of the Doha Development Agenda. One year on, I am of course very keen to see and learn how world_trade_organization implementation is happening in practice, so I want to hear your views on that, and in particular what impacts you have noticed here in Guangzhou. And I would also like to discuss the new world_trade_organization Round, which was also of course launched last year in Doha, because I think it is an excellent example of how the european_union and China can, should, indeed must work together in the new global economy.  world_trade_organization accession: implementation  But let me start with a pivotal, perhaps the pivotal question, and that is an assessment of how China is managing thus far in the world_trade_organization. Seen from Brussels, the overall assessment is a positive one.  I say "overall" because the picture is not 100% rosy. There remain some serious difficulties, but on balance the positives are bigger than the negatives. We are following with great interest the enormous efforts China is making to put in place a stronger legislative framework, whether this is in relation to intellectual property, or services, or at the technical level, the structure for quota and tariff-rate quota management, etc. There is no doubt in my mind that China and her leadership is extremely eager to demonstrate that China will respect her world_trade_organization obligations in full.  Of course, from the perspective of european_union industry and indeed the export sector in China, the key is how these commitments are being translated into action on the ground in terms of your ability to carry out your day to day business. I fully recognise that even implementing the letter of world_trade_organization commitments is not easy to do, particularly in a vast and diverse country like China. But abiding by the spirit of world_trade_organization rules will be even more of a challenge. By this, I mean that it is relatively straightforward - from an administrative viewpoint - to deliver lower tariffs, for example. Much harder to set in place the necessary mechanisms and structure to ensure full transparency and predictability. I fully recognise that this means for China (as it has meant for others) a step change in day to day business practice.  There are a number of short term issues to address. For example, if China is still to attract such a major share of inward investment, she needs to consider carefully the way foreign companies are treated in one or two areas. Clearly, companies will only open new branches, for example, if they feel they are going to be profitable. But such decisions are coloured, for example in the banking sector, by the requirements for new capital of 100 million RMB per licence, and no fewer than 6 licenses are needed to cover the full scope of banking activities. Of course as a former banker, I never question legitimate prudential concerns, and I would be the last to do so in China case, which is after all still developing its banking sector. But I have to wonder if these requirements are not disproportionate, when you consider that only € 5 million is required to establish in the european_union a subsidiary benefiting from the single banking passport.  Allied to these teething problems is the transitional nature of a lot of the Chinese commitments in the world_trade_organization. A lot of the benefits only come on stream over a period of several years, in the services sector (banking, insurance) as well as for goods (with full trading rights being granted only 3 years after accession). Of course, I know this well, having negotiated some of these provisions myself only two or so years ago. But it is not always easy for either Chinese or European industry - when the true position will not be clear (in some sectors) for the next three to five years. And as Zhu Rongji said to me himself, China still has to take a decision - herself - on the next stage of liberalisation in sectors such as telecoms and insurance.  But I also want to stress that we are taking a distinctively European approach to problems of world_trade_organization accession. For example, in the ongoing discussions in Geneva on Chinese world_trade_organization accession under the so-called Transitional Review Mechanism, our approach has been to avoid taking a narrow legalistic approach, but to address problems in a rather positive way with China. We are focused on results, and concrete actions by the Chinese side, such as on protection of intellectual property rights, for example. It is one thing to ensure that the framework of legislation is in line with international standards, and quite another to ensure that enforcement, particularly against piracy and counterfeiting, is rigorously pursued, and this is of course another area where wer are very keen to see action from the Chinese side.  Finally, in terms of the longer term, we will not forget the importance of technical assistance, ., the financial support Europe gives to China in order to improve its implementation of world_trade_organization commitments.  I have argued in the past that the date of Chinese accession was not so much the finishing line as the starting line in terms of meeting world_trade_organization standards. The european_union is absolutely committed to helping China in this difficult transitional period. We are currently finalising the design of a major new programme, worth 15 million euro, which together with a Chinese contribution of 5.6 million euro, makes up the largest technical assistance programme supporting China integration into the international trading system. In terms of current or recent co-operation projects with China, this adds up to european_union support of around € 22M.  And - final word on this - what does this mean in terms of the final stage, of going to world_trade_organization dispute settlement proceedings if we do not manage to solve a particular dispute ? Some have speculated that we will not want to "upset" China by going to dispute settlement resolution. Well this is untrue. I am absolutely ready to go to the world_trade_organization where other courses of action have produced no effective results. But we dont, I think, help matters by dragging China into the world_trade_organization at the first sign of a problem. Our preferred approach is to recognise the size of the challenge, and to try to build confidence in the systems, both in the european_union and China, to facilitate trade. Of course I am ready to go to the world_trade_organization if necessary, but only if other methods fail.  To conclude on this point, we are keen of course that China should implement faithfully her world_trade_organization commitments, and we want to help in this regard. Overall, as I said in the beginning, the signs are pointing in the right direction: not least because Zhu Rongji himself believes, and has said in the past, that world_trade_organization accession directly assists China own internal reform programme, and sets China firmly on the path towards sustainable development.  The Doha Development Agenda  Perhaps I could now turn to the second dish on this afternoon menu, and that is the Doha Development Agenda, the new Round of trade negotiations in the world_trade_organization. And this, too, I believe to be good news for China.  This statement might at first appear a little odd. Why should China, after 15 years' long march into the world_trade_organization, after many years of tough bargaining to establish the price of its world_trade_organization entry ticket, wish to be immediately plunged again into fresh negotiations, in the first new multilateral round of trade talks we have launched since 1986 ? But I am absolutely delighted that the Chinese leadership has so quickly grasped the significance of what is at stake in the Doha Development Agenda that we launched in November of last year. This project is of huge, huge significance at the present time.  First, the economic prognosis is not promising. We face difficult times ahead in the world economy, difficulties which frankly a war in the Middle East will only serve to exacerbate. So it is even more important, in this context, that the world_trade_organization comes up with rather rapid results to stimulate medium term growth and improve economic stability.  But secondly, a crucial part of this is not just the further liberalisation of market access - both for China and other countries: we also have to take steps to improve the rules which govern world trade. We have to keep the world_trade_organization relevant: it is no good plunging ahead with trade liberalisation on a global level if the global rules dont ensure that the notional market access generated by a tariff cut become effective on the ground.  In short, we have to keep the world_trade_organization relevant, and I am pleased to say that I have found the Chinese remarkably open to European ideas in the doha_development_agenda, and I hope vice versa.  We have, in Geneva, and between capitals, started to develop a very close working relationship on all the key issues, and I think we have found many common interests. We are impressed by the quality of Chinese contributions to the debates in Geneva, by the level of commitment to the doha_development_agenda not just in the world_trade_organization but also in regional forums such as the ASEM and APEC, and by the practical and businesslike solutions which China is putting forward, for the most part. Indeed, our common commitment to this was well put at the recent european_union-China summit in Copenhagen, where both the european_union and China stressed "their shared interest in strengthening the multilateral trading system".  So my message to you is that we are intensifying our work with the Chinese government, and we are both very keen to put together an ambitious result in the Round, a point reiterated to me only yesterday by Madame Wu Yi, and Minister Shi Guangsheng, in Beijing yesterday.  As to the detail, we are now assembling a major bilateral dialogue with China on the new Round, and a team of world_trade_organization experts will be flying out to Beijing next month to launch that process. We are particularly keen to work on a number of areas of interest, such as trade defence instruments and safeguards (where I think we can both agree that the rules need updating), technical barriers to trade, and agriculture. We also see good prospects for working together on the so-called new issues such as investment, public procurement, trade facilitation, and competition.  These are of course vital issues for both the european_union and China. The way forward is to develop market access but underpinned by better multilateral rules in order to attract good quality investment, and to improve the quality of public administration. Progress in these areas means more stability and predictability for companies, lower costs of trade transactions, and better export performance. In terms of the current needs of the ailing world economy, it is not surprising that we are seeing the importance of these issues in the Doha Development Round rising up the political agenda.  But perhaps most of all, in the Doha Development Agenda, China has an early opportunity to pursue the ideas and principles that she has set out on the world stage. To be very frank, I think we need to recognise that the world_trade_organization negotiating process was rather a painful and difficult process for China, a seemingly never ending one-way street of demands on China for market access, changes in her domestic legislation and so on. But in the doha_development_agenda, the traffic flows in two directions and China can herself seek to derive direct benefit from the negotiations.  And not just China will benefit from this two way flow, but the doha_development_agenda talks as a whole will benefit. China is starting to emerge as a key player in the debate on global governance, a key pole in the economic system. China understands that we need a broad and ambitious outcome to the Doha negotiations, and an outcome covering both rules and market access.  Conclusion  Last year saw the launch of a new era for China and the world_trade_organization, a new era which sees China emerging rapidly as one of the key poles of the world economy. This year, as we get down to business, all the signs are that China remains very much on track both economically and politically to claiming her rightful place at the global economic top table.  The message I take from this year is that China is committed to delivering on the terms of her world_trade_organization accession package, and to playing a very full part in the Doha Development Agenda.  But in addition, close co-operation and collaboration between the european_union and the Chinese authorities is going to be increasingly vital in terms of moving our trade relationship forward.  The fact is that - together - the european_union and China can make an enormous contribution to keeping markets open, to keeping the world_trade_organization steaming ahead, to strengthening global economic governance, and to building sustainable development. And together - we can bridge the debate in the world_trade_organization between north and south.  After all, if the european_union trade policy, in stressing the importance of the development dimension, looks north and south, China, as a rapidly developing country, has one foot in the north and one in the south.  Thank you very much: I believe we still have time for your questions and comments, so please fire away.

title: The Rt Hon Chris Patten Commissioner for External Relations China and Europe an important partnership Cathay Pacific China Trader Award Dinner Kasteel De Wittenberg, 6 November 2002
text: The Fundaments of european_union-China relations  Diplomatic relations with China established in 1975. There have been dramatic changes in China since 1978. Economic and social reforms have made China a major trading nation and emerging global political power. EC-China Trade and Co-operation Agreement in 1985. Since then relations have accelerated and intensified, particularly in recent years, building on a series of Commission strategy papers (1995, 1998, 2001) which define the long term policy for the relationship. european_union main objective: to build a dynamic and comprehensive partnership, support China rapid and full integration into the international community, politically and economically. China success will be beneficial for all. The european_union encourages China to take its full place in the institutions of global governance. european_union-China relations have developed under three main headings, namely: political dialogue, which includes specific dialogue on human rights; economic and trade relations, including relations in a number of sectors which are key to economic and social reform, european_union-China Co-operation Programme, an instrument through which the european_union provides technical assistance to China. european_union-China Relations present status and plans for the near future  Economic and Trade Relations  Economic and trade relations were the starting point, and continue to be a very important facet, of our relationship. In 1975, China barely appeared on our map. In 2001, there was bilateral trade of €105 bn and China was the 3rd largest trading partner outside of Europe. This growth trend continues, with more than €62 bn in trade in the first 7 months of 2002. The impact of world_trade_organization membership is clear: european_union exports to China in 2002 grew more strongly than imports (10.9% against 2.9%). We hope that accession will reduce the huge european_union trade deficit, which reached €45 billion in 2001, the largest with any trading partner. Reasons for this deficit include the growing strength of China as an exporter. european_union companies present in China contribute significantly to this, as do the continuance of market access obstacles. China accession to the world_trade_organization was one of the most momentous events of recent times, and of crucial importance in removing the remaining barriers, to promote increased european_union investment. There are increasing amounts of european_union investment in China ($25bn in mid-2001). But european_union share of the total is far smaller than elsewhere. In terms of new flows, the european_union is down from 1st to 4th place (excluding HK) in 2000. european_union investors seem to be holding back, while others (united_states, Japan, Taiwan) are seizing new opportunities following world_trade_organization entry. As a result, China is poised to overtake the united_states as the largest foreign_direct_investment recipient in the world in 2002. european_union companies should be less timid and ensure strong European presence in the Chinese market. The european_union has always been a keen supporter of Chinese accession to the world_trade_organization. China membership will benefit both China and the world - cementing China place in the global economy, buttressing the internal reform process, and strengthening the rule of law. The european_union will seek to work closely with China to make the Doha Development Agenda a success. A successful conclusion will be crucial to the development of global economic exchanges, particularly in the present economic climate. The european_union and China will play important roles, not least as potential bridge-builders between developed and developing countries. The monitoring of China world_trade_organization compliance will be a major european_union priority in coming years, to ensure that our firms benefit fully. We recognise that China has achieved a lot since accession. But issues of concern remain, including lack of transparency and establishment of new non-trade barriers which could neutralise liberalisation brought by accession. Problems are to some extent unavoidable, given the immensity of the task with which China is faced. Yet we will seek to resolve these through consultations, and by supporting China through dialogue and co-operation programmes. China integration into the world economy and continued internal reforms will not be painless. There will be enormous challenges: economic - the requirement to maintain growth and overall macro-economic stability; social - growing income disparities, increasing urban unemployment and tensions in the countryside; political - the need to manage these complex processes, and at the same time, gradually put into place the rule of law. Reforms are on the right track. The main thrust of european_union-China cooperation programmes are to support China in this challenging process. Co-operation strategies therefore support implementation of the european_union general strategy towards China, rather than focussing on traditional development assistance. In particular, assistance focusses on the considerable european_union experience on how to adapt rules and policies to globalisation, and how to minimise the negative socio-economic impact. The european_union would like to share this information with China, to help ease her integration into the global system. This is a key objective of european_union-China dialogue, and of several european_union cooperation projects, which will continue to be reinforced. Much progress has been made in stepping up dialogue on questions of sectoral policy and regulation, focussing on world_trade_organization-/reform-related areas, . enterprise policy, standardisation, information society, environmental policy, energy, and science and technology.  The european_union is seeking to complement the 1985 Agreement by negotiating a number of bilateral agreements. A maritime transport agreement is to be signed later this year. There are ongoing discussions on a customs cooperation agreement, to facilitate trade and combat counterfeiting. Additional agreements may be possible in the future. The european_union will also seek to strengthen cooperation with China in other important areas, such as education and science and technology. The GALILEO project combines scientific and industrial aspects, in which China has expressed keen interest. The european_union will explore ways of making China a privileged partner in this important venture. For full integration of China into the international community, it is not enough that Chinese laws and regulations change, mentalities must change too. This will require long term effort in training and dissemination of information. The Commission is ready to play a key role in this respect. The european_union will contribute €67 million over the next 3 years through relevant cooperation projects, including support in the areas of world_trade_organization obligations, enterprise reform, social security reform, financial services and the information society. These new activities complement ongoing programmes on intellectual_property_rights, world_trade_organization accession, legal and judicial cooperation. Looking beyond trade: european_union-China political and human rights dialogue  world_trade_organization membership in the medium-term will have a profound impact on Chinese society as a whole. world_trade_organization rules oblige the government to provide judicial review for administrative actions in the economic realm. This is likely to have spill-over effects throughout the legal system and help consolidate the rule of law. world_trade_organization entry will thus support China transition towards an open society based on the rule of law and respect for human rights. This is a key european_union objective. Respect for human rights will benefit China development and lead to greater stability. It is the european_union duty, as a friend of China and of the Chinese, to be open and candid on this important issue. Human rights dialogue, held twice a year, was established in 1996. This enables discussion of european_union concerns in areas such as: freedom of expression, association and religion, application of torture and death penalty and the situation of ethnic minorities, in particular in Tibet and Xinjiang. Individual cases are also raised. The next round of dialogue will be on 14/15 November in Beijing. Human rights dialogue is complemented by a series of cooperation projects . support to Chinese implementation of the UN human rights covenants, democratisation at local level and legal and judicial reform. A human rights small project facility, which aims to support emerging Chinese civil society, was launched this year. Chinese civil society will play a key role in successful transition towards a more open society. The european_union will therefore seek its deeper involvement in both dialogue and co-operation programmes. Human rights is only one part of the regular and intensive european_union-China political dialogue. There have been five annual summits since 1998. Political dialogue has broadened considerably in recent years, and now covers a wide range of topics, from regional conflict to global issues such as non-proliferation and international terrorism. The european_union would like to engage China further in addressing the "dark side of globalisation" . illegal migration/trafficking in human beings, drug trafficking and money-laundering. Illegal migration is a relatively new area of co-operation, which gained momentum after the 2000 Dover tragedy, when high-level dialogue on the issue was established. A €10 million co-operation programme is currently under discussion, which would strengthen Chinese capacity to combat illegal migration, raise awareness of key target groups and improve information/ support research in this area. Speedy return of illegal migrants is key to any policy on illegal migration; we therefore intend to start exploratory talks for an eventual readmission agreement ASAP. On the other hand, legal personal contacts are also essential to broaden and deepen the relationship. The european_union is committed to increasing such contacts through exchanges in both directions. It will therefore be important to conclude the agreement on "Authorised Destination Status" ASAP, which will facilitate and multiply visits of Chinese tourist groups to the european_union. Political dialogue also covers Hong Kong and Macau. The european_union watches developments closely and monitors compliance with Basic Laws, one country, two systems'. HK is the showcase for what world_trade_organization membership and economic liberalisation, and also the rule of law could mean for China. The HK anti-sedition law is a worrying step in the wrong direction. Finally, in our political dialogue, we also discuss the thorny issue of Taiwan. There is a clear european_union stake in stability and peace across the Taiwan Straits and a successful Taiwanese transition from an authoritarian to a democratic regime. There are strong european_union commercial links with Taiwan, which is our 3rd largest trading partner in Asia. The european_union position is One China' but the Taiwan issue can only be resolved through peaceful dialogue, without the use or threat of force. Rapid economic integration of Taiwan with the Mainland will bring the two sides closer together. Direct transport and trade links will help and we should welcome recent positive noises on this (although some in this room have a clear stake in the status quo). Conclusion  The 5th european_union-China Summit was successfully concluded on 24 September. The Summit broad agenda demonstrated a mature partnership. Both sides stressed their resolve to further expand and deepen european_union-China co-operation in all areas to strengthen this partnership, including by jointly addressing global challenges like migration. Trade and economic relations will be an essential ingredient of our relations. But we have moved far beyond the initial one-dimensional stage of our relationship. The european_union is changing in ways that are leading China to adjust its own strategic vision of Europe. The Euro, european_union enlargement, ESDP and CFSP are all ingredients of an ever-increasing interest in strengthening Sino-European relations. The european_union and China increasingly exchange views and co-ordinate positions on international issues and global challenges, such as terrorism, non-proliferation and regional conflicts. We hope to intensify and expand policy co-ordination in the future. The european_union is building on China desire for a global role, by encouraging it to make a deeper commitment to world affairs in the emerging 21st Century global order. Many political and economic uncertainties lie ahead for China. There is a profound european_union interest in a stable, prosperous and open China that fully embraces democracy, free market principles and the rule of law. Developing a comprehensive, robust and enduring relationship with China will be one of the european_union great challenges in the 21st century. This will also be central to China playing its full part on the world stage. There had been a very positive start, relations are at a historic high point, as confirmed by President Jiang earlier this year. But much more can, and should, be done. The european_union is prepared to work with China, in the framework of its strategic partnership, to achieve a lasting and mutually beneficial relationship for generations to come.

title: Pascal Lamy EU Trade Commissioner EU's enlargement will contribute to the deepening of EU-China relations EU Chamber of Commerce in China Beijing, China, 13 June 2003
text: Ladies and Gentlemen,  Well, it is great to be back. It feels like a lot more than eight months since I was here, to be honest. You and indeed China have had a tough, tough time. Coverage of the SARS epidemic in the foreign press set aside, it is of course now vital to ensure that there is no lasting effect on China economy and business interests. Perhaps the most amazing thing is how completely the Chinese economy has rebounded, with growth levels touching if anything new heights in the first quarter of this year, even if we should expect some effect on growth overall in 2003.  But in a way it is much less than eight months, because I had the pleasure to receive a visit from President Behrens and representatives of the Working Groups in Brussels only last March. This level of frequency in our meetings demonstrates, if there was ever a need to, just how effective, indeed essential, the EUCCC has become. In just a few short years, you have gone from excited launch to established institution: you are of course now our main business interlocutor on Chinese trade relations. So SARS or no SARS, I at very least owed you a return visit.  This rather rapid visit to China was meant to be my main trip of the year, built around the annual festivities of the Joint Committee. Unfortunately this has been postponed due to the SARS outbreak, but I am delighted to able to meet the new Chinese Government for the first time, in particular Premier Wen Jiabao. I was deeply impressed by his rather steely determination to continue pressing on with China reforms, and with integrating the Chinese economy globally. But it is also a very strong signal that the European Union remains every bit as important a partner for the new Chinese government as under the previous one.  For my own part, I stressed that our bilateral relations are essential at a difficult time for China due to SARS and I personally passed to him Romano Prodi warm greetings and expressions of deepest solidarity. But China cannot rely just on warm words, but also needs hard cash. So I am very pleased that the european_union has rapidly mobilised around 10 million € on SARS related cooperation and related research and development.  I was also able to update Premier Wen on the european_union forthcoming enlargement and reassured him that the incorporation of 10 new countries, long-time friends of China, will help to deepen european_union-China relations and improve our bilateral trade. It is of course true that we all have to work hard to persuade China that this is an extremely positive development for her.  I was also pleased to renew my acquaintance with Minister Li from AQSIQ, the General Administration of Quality Supervision, Inspection and Quarantine. We have agreed with Minister Li to strengthen our contacts in the future to resolve the many remaining technical issues which we have in the areas of responsibility of AQSIQ. But, more importantly, I was particularly pleased to learn the good news from him that China is now lifting the ban on Dutch imports, a long standing problem. Obviously a definitive lifting of the ban can only take place based on the results of technical inspections these are scheduled for the end of June. But this clearly shows that common work and sustained efforts are essential to tackle trade issues and that they can bring results.  Finally, I will have the chance to meet tomorrow with Mr. Lu Fuyuan, the new Minister of Commerce, who I am delighted to welcome onto the travelling circus of world_trade_organization trade ministers, as we tread a rather weary, but still optimistic, path to the next world_trade_organization Ministerial meeting in Cancun and the end of the world_trade_organization Round. We will review together the overall state of Sino-european_union trade relations and prepare for the busy autumn ahead, featuring Cancun, and the world_trade_organization Trade review of Chinese commitments in Geneva, not to mention the next european_union-China Summit in Beijing.  So let me share with you my views on the overall state of our bilateral relationship with China. I will touch on three dimensions of our trade relations: implementation, negotiation and cooperation.  One and a half years after China entry into the world_trade_organization, where do we stand? When I was here in October I said that our overall assessment on China implementation of her commitments was a positive one. I maintain that assessment, although we also need to keep our eyes open and react when necessary. It was a vast and sprawling negotiation, and we should not be surprised that it is a vast work to monitor and track Chinese implementation. On this subject in particular, I want to hear your views, the views of the European business community.  The first year was largely devoted to successfully putting into place the vast amount of new legislation required for implementing the Chinese commitments.  In some instances we have seen some difficulties, related to delays, lack of clarity or apparent discrepancies with the world_trade_organization agreement. One or two examples we have unearthed which are grounds for concern. For instance, there is a one and a half years delay in passing the legislation to allow car companies to offer purchases on credit to Chinese consumers. Likewise, the newly-published rules on mergers and acquisitions introduce a rather vague mechanism controlling the purchase of local companies by foreign ones: the criteria are anything but clear. Also, China is allowing foreign companies to engage in import/export throughout China but the new regulation includes some requirements that did not figure in the Protocol of accession. Something similar occurs in the telecom sector. We need to clarify all this.  But my overall view remains positive. We are not in Brussels overwhelmed with complaints and concerns about Chinese failure to implement its legislative commitments. These instances above are generally the exception to the rule: China has delivered legislation that is mostly rather in line with what we expected. Probably the area where this is most visible is on tariffs. As planned, duties are being reduced year after year, and this has led to what is currently the most tangible economic benefit in terms of market access.  Nevertheless, the picture is a bit more mixed when we look at it from the ground. Implementation in practice is not easy, and no world_trade_organization Member can claim to have an entirely clean record in this respect. In my view, we need to focus on the following elements to reduce the gap between law and practice:  Industry and consumers should be governed by law. The wide discretion that is still in the hands of the public authorities should be reduced, as far as possible, to the international standards in modern economies. All too often, unclear legislation is combined with excessive discretion. For instance, the current management of Chinese import quotas has led to the appearance of a parallel market for licenses, particularly for fertilisers and automobiles. Full transparency is also necessary when regulators consult industry on draft legislation, to avoid the risk that only some companies getting the chance to provide comments. Great if you are picked out: not so good if you are left out in the cold.  There is still a tendency to sometimes favour local companies over foreign ones. In this respect, the establishment of a one-stop Ministry of Commerce, dealing with both domestic and external trade, is to be welcomed since this will hopefully lower the risk of discrimination. But problems will remain in some areas such as in financial services, where capital requirements reinforce the dominant position of Chinese State-owned banks and insurers against foreign players. Although China considers them as prudential requirements, we view them as excessive.  For instance, banks in China need 72 million € to enjoy the same scope of business than we grant in the european_union with just 5 million €. There is no inherent reason why this should be so. And it has an immediate effect on market access.  The wide discretion granted to the administration sometimes leads to inconsistencies between different provinces and municipalities when they apply the law. This adds to the level of uncertainty. A clear example is in the implementation of standards by different customs offices or the protection of intellectual property rights.  So, looking at implementation on the ground, it is certainly easier to spot difficulties in the texts of legislation than in their practical implementation. It is in the latter instances that the role of business is even more important.  The Working groups of the Chamber are now busy preparing the updates of their Position Papers. I cannot stress strongly enough how important it is to continue ensuring good quality Papers. As you know, they have become a key and rather prominent tool for the Commission when assessing the implementation of Chinese commitments.  So I am delighted to see that the Chamber is growing in this respect, and is becoming even more professional, partly thanks to the decisive support of some corporate sponsors. The incorporation of business managers to help the Working Groups, which make up the core of the Chamber, will certainly help to increase awareness of the objectives of european_union business in China. And of course, the Delegation of the European Commission will continue to work hand in glove with the Chamber.  To conclude on this point, let me remind you that from October to December the world_trade_organization will conduct its second annual Trade Review on the implementation of Chinese commitments. The first time took place during a year of transition. This second one should show progress and prepare the ground for the third year, when essential Chinese commitments will enter into force, such as on distribution or trading rights. Our approach is to be firm but fair in this: it is not right to use this mechanism to hound the Chinese unfairly in Geneva. But we can and should use it to raise clearly identified problems.  Finally, let me turn to Geneva and the world_trade_organization Doha Development Agenda. I know that current world_trade_organization negotiations in Geneva may seem far away for you - but as good businessmen, you should always keep an eye on the long distance weather forecasts. As with Chinese accession, long term weather forecasts become today weather sooner than we sometimes expect. Let me also tell you briefly where we stand on this.  Concluding the round by the end of 2004 remains our priority. For this the next Ministerial Conference in September in Cancun must be a success. For Cancun, the key areas where agreements are needed are:  On agriculture, industrial market access, investment, competition, trade facilitation and government procurement, we need to decide on the negotiating modalities. We also need to put together a package of results with real value added for developing countries and making further progress after Cancun. On Geographical Indications, Trade and environment and Dispute Settlement, Cancun should start delivering results, albeit sometimes in rather moderate ways, and point towards the final outcome. To attain what remains a rather high level of ambition, the european_union will need to continue to force the pace, and we hope others, including China, will join united_states in the vanguard. Doha, of course, was the conclusion of China accession negotiations and the start of a new round of trade negotiations.  As a large and influential world_trade_organization Member, China also carries responsibility for making this round a success in order to bolster economic growth and strengthen multilateralism. More prosaically, it is only with an ambitious position that China will be able to get others to "pay", as she herself has done, in the accession process.  We understand that China still remains hesitant to make new market access commitments but feel that China, frankly, could be ready to do rather more in working to strengthen the rules aspects of the world_trade_organization. This will advance the multilateral agenda that we both share, an agenda which is the only choice in this multipolar world. In a way, Cancun will be the first real test for assessing China commitment with the world_trade_organization system. For my part, I am extremely confident that the Chinese government will play a very and constructive role, and I look forward to discussing the upcoming mini-Ministerial meeting in Egypt, which takes place only next week, with Minister Lu.  Before I conclude, a brief word on how we can best pursue all the objectives I have just described, using what I can only describe as a formidable tool in the shape of its cooperation programs. With a current budget of 22 million € for various trade-related projects, the european_union is the top donor in terms of trade assistance to China. A new 15 million € project, just signed a couple of days ago, will be implemented for the five years to come.  With the same logic, since regulatory issues have a growing importance in our bilateral relations, we are working to establish bilateral regulatory dialogues on issues such as industrial standards, conformity assessment, etc. The objective is to use early exchanges of views to ensure we avoid the emergence of new problems in the future.  The first part of my conclusion is unescapable: China is clearly taking advantage of its integration in the world_trade_organization and the world economy. She has become, extraordinarily quickly, the world 4th largest trader (after the united_states, the european_union and Japan), its surplus with the world keeps growing, and it is now the top destination for foreign_direct_investment (ahead of the united_states). Such advantages keep China economy booming.  On our side, in 2002 China became the european_union 3rd largest trading partner, overtaking Japan. There has been a striking upwards trend in the first three months of 2003. Trade has grown by 20% (more than twice that if you prefer the Chinese statistics) and european_union foreign_direct_investment in China has increased by a staggering 80%, again according to Chinese statistics.  Of course, in parallel with this amazing performance, China has to keep in line with its commitments, and particularly her pledge to grant effective and non-discriminatory access to the Chinese market.  As european_union-China trade ties continue to deepen, as our interdependence grows and grows, as our political relationship blossoms, the european_union and China are becoming ever closer partners. We can see that in the management of multilateral trade. We both have similar interests in multilaterally strengthening the world_trade_organization system. At the same time we will both pursue our respective bilateral trade interests to ensure the best possible level of mutual market access. That is natural, that is the path of mature partners.  And in both the stewardship of multilateral rights and obligations, and a vigorous yet productive bilateral relationship, we will continue to depend heavily on European business in China - and we know we can continue to count on the European Chamber.  Thank you very much. Happy to take questions.

title: Progress in EU-China relations at summit in Beijing
text: Progress in european_union-China relations at summit in Beijing  At the 6th Annual european_union-China Summit, which takes place on 30 October in Beijing, the European Union and China intend to conclude two major new cooperation agreements on satellite navigation and tourism facilitation and a new dialogue on industrial policy. The parties will discuss topics including human rights, the fight against illegal migration, trade and investment relations, technical cooperation and international concerns.  Ahead of the Summit, President Romano Prodi said "european_union-China relations have expanded rapidly in recent years. As the european_union enlarges and China continues to emerge on the world stage, our bilateral and international agenda is more challenging than ever and this summit affords a major opportunity to move forward together."  It is intended to conclude two major agreements at the summit, opening new areas of cooperation between the european_union and China. The agreement on Galileo would enable China to participate in this strategic European satellite navigation programme, notably through a substantial financial stakeholding in the Galileo joint undertaking. The agreement on Authorised Destination Status would facilitate visa procedures for group travel from China. This would allow more Chinese tourists to visit the Union which will promote people-to-people contacts between China and the european_union.  The newly established dialogue on industrial policy will also be concluded at the summit. This dialogue aims at ensuring a business-friendly level playing field for industrial operations and at contributing to smooth and sustainable trade relations between the european_union and China. The european_union will also express its desire to start negotiations on a customs cooperation agreement.  The summit will welcome the recent release of both european_union and China policy papers on european_union-China relations, which provide the way ahead for the development of relations in the years to come. This is China first ever policy paper on the relations with european_union, something which we have recognised as testimony to the importance attached by China to our relationship.  The european_union side will underline the need to reinforce the european_union-China Human Rights Dialogue and make it more results-oriented and raise issues of particular concern to the european_union in the field of human rights. Leaders will exchange views on how the european_union and China can contribute to the resolution of regional conflicts, notably through supporting efforts to counter proliferation of weapons of mass destruction in the Korean peninsula and to promote the peace process in the Middle East. Special attention will also be paid to the current political situation in Burma/Myanmar as well as to the international cooperation for the reconstruction of Iraq. The european_union will stress the need for further joint efforts to combat illegal migration and trafficking in human beings, including the need to sign a readmission agreement and for practical cooperation in this field.  The European Union will be represented by the Italian Prime Minister, Silvio Berlusconi, in his role as the President of the European Council, the President of the European Commission, Romano Prodi and the Secretary General of the Council and High Representative for Common Foreign and Security Policy, Javier Solana. The Italian Minister for Foreign Affairs, Franco Frattini, and the European Commissioner for Trade, Pascal Lamy, will also participate.  The Chinese side will be led by Prime Minister Wen Jiabao, who will be accompanied by a delegation of Ministers, Vice-Ministers and officials, including the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Li Zhaoxing, Minister for Science and Technology, Xu Guanhua and Chairman of the National Development and Reform Commission, Ma Kai.  Prior to the Summit, European leaders will have a bilateral meeting with Chinese President Hu Jintao.  The summit will be preceded by a meeting of the european_union-China Joint Committee at Ministerial level where european_union Trade Commissioner Pascal Lamy will have an opportunity to discuss trade issue with his Chinese counterpart. High on the agenda will be a discussion on the implementation by China of world_trade_organization commitments as well as the enlargement of the european_union and its impact on european_union-China trade relations.  Background:  The first european_union-China Annual Summit took place in London in April 1998; followed by the second and the third, both in Beijing, in December 1999 and October 2000, and the fourth in Brussels in September 2001. Last summit in September 2002 in Copenhagen reaffirmed both sides' will to reinforce exchanges.  On 10 September 2003, the European Commission issued a Communication on China entitled, "A maturing partnership - Common interests and challenges in european_union-China Relations". This document, which was endorsed by the european_union General Affairs Council of 13 October 2003, charts a course for further deepening of relations in the medium term and includes a number of concrete measures to this end. On the Chinese side, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs published also, on 13 October 2003, its first ever policy paper on the relations with european_union, something which we have recognized as testimony to the importance attached by China to our relationship.  China is the european_union third largest trading partner, after the USA and Switzerland. The main european_union imports from China consist of machinery, transport equipment, textiles and chemicals.

title: Pascal Lamy EU Commissioner for Trade EU-China relations: continuity and change EU Chamber of Commerce in China Beijing, 31 October 2003
text: Introduction  Thank you very much Serge Janssens de Varebeke and good to be back here, just four months after my last visit. Once again, let me take the opportunity to thank you for all your efforts, and for the remarkable way in which the Chamber and the European Commission are working together, not just in the twenty Working Groups, but across the board in european_union-China trade relations.  Before I begin my substantive remarks, a few words about what I have been doing during another rewarding, but again fairly brief visit. Yesterday, I co-chaired the european_union-China Joint Committee with Vice Minister Lei in the regrettable absence through illness of Minister Lu, and together with Romano Prodi, paid a call on President Hu before we took part in the european_union-China summit chaired on the Chinese side by Wen Jiabao. We signed plenty of agreements, including two new dialogues (on intellectual property and industrial policy both I should think of interest to the Chamber) and the important ADS agreement which will be beneficial to the tourist industry, and of course the Galileo agreement. As a night-cap, I took part in the evening in my first ever Internet Chat in China at the People Daily, which was a great experience, and is emblematic of the extraordinary pace of change in China. Thence to the airport for a 12 hour flight to New York, ahead of the New York marathon, which takes place on Sunday. In fact I think it is a pretty good excuse which I shall of course use to explain why I did not run 2hrs 30 minutes.  european_union-China bilateral trade relations  I think it is useful that the Chamber has chosen this provocative title for my talk today because sometimes it is important to pose fundamental questions. For all those who thought Chinese trade relations with the rest of the world would become less an issue of interest and controversy, the current surge of interest, notably in Washington, must come as a real surprise. But: plus ca change, plus cest la meme chose…perhaps the most startling thing about our current trading relationship with China is that it has been marked, during my four years as Trade Commissioner, by extraordinary change, but it is also hard to avoid the conclusion that the fundamental positives and negatives, the underlying assumptions, the questions, remain broadly the same.  So: are we making real strides in our trade relationship with China ? Or are we stumbling backwards ? In my remarks today, I would like to begin with the issues which are perhaps of most interest to you - ., addressing european_union-China trade disputes. But I also want to give you some insight into how we are addressing our differences on multilateral trade policy questions with China in the world_trade_organization.  If we are to talk about change, let united_states begin in an obvious place. It is interesting to recall that in 1999, the total size of China trade with the rest of the world was no greater than that of the Netherlands. Four years on, China is well on the way to becoming our second largest trade partner: our second largest source of imports [and of course closing fast on the united_states]; and our fifth largest export outlet. Total trade is now worth €115 billion, and is growing faster than the rocket China recently despatched into orbit - at around 17% a year. But of course it is impossible to ignore the fact that our trade deficit is now an amazing 40% of the size of total trade, and this too is growing at a rather healthy rate.  Indeed, has the european_union trade deficit now become a political problem, as it evidently has in the united_states ? In my view, not yet. Why ? First, because the european_union has perhaps less of a mercantilist vision which demands at least a balance of trade, and preferably a positive balance, with each of its partners. Secondly, however, the european_union has an overall balance of trade, more or less: obviously a deficit with China, but a surplus elsewhere. Thirdly, there is quite a degree of understanding as to the unique circumstances behind China extraordinary competitive position at the moment. For example, the advantage coming from the annual graduation of more than 550,000 engineers cannot be overlooked. The combination of very high levels of foreign investment, cheap but well-trained labour, and a middle class which while growing fast is not yet sucking in the same level of imports as developed economies is hard to overstate. Finally, because - unlike in the united_states - there is a much lesser sense of geopolitical challenge to Europe from China. Partly that is because Europe is not yet a political superpower herself. But partly it is because we are happy to see a future in which at the same time as we safeguard a strong transatlantic alliance, we see a greater diffusion of power, a more multi-polar world, developing.  So the european_union is relatively immune to the kind of rather difficult politics in the united_states at the moment on this topic. But I say only "relatively". Clear that there is more pressure mounting on this front, particularly in certain member states, and we should not be too nonchalant about that.  But descending from these lofty geopolitical heights, at which we can look with relative equanimity at the amazing advance of the Chinese economy, particularly the exporting part of it, let me just add that it would be good if Chinese policy makers were to at least acknowledge the trade deficit in the sense of recognising the great openness of the European economy, and the absence of structural barriers to trade. To hear complaints of damaging discrimination against Chinese firms (for example in trade defence) at the same time as the trade gap is widening by 15% a year in the other direction is stretching the bounds of credulity, to be honest. And doubly so when the complaints are often cast in rather Chinese nationalistic terms, which is unfortunate as it makes it harder to find solutions to the problems.  And returning to our themes of continuity and change, there seems to be one constant in our enormous and rapidly growing trade and investment relationship with China: trade problems. We are not surprised or shocked by this, given the pace of change in the relationship as a whole. Our equanimity in the face of the Chinese deficit does not mean that we are in any way indifferent to problems european_union companies have in trading with and investing in China. Our record shows that we are ready to address each and every issue, point by point, with the Chinese authorities. Here, I am particularly grateful both to the Chamber and to our own Delegation here in Beijing for the excellent role they play in bringing problems rapidly to our attention. Indeed, many of the issues raised by the Chamber in your recent Position Papers which we have received and digested back in Brussels have now been identified formally as priority issues. These we will now take up in a very direct but constructive way with the authorities here in Beijing, and indeed many of the topics have come up in the last day or so while we have been here.  Let me say a word or two about each type of problems in turn, starting with the problems where we are most able to move forward, coming down to the hardest.  The most obvious area stems specifically from world_trade_organization implementation problems, but we are of course more than ready to take up issues which go beyond Chinese world_trade_organization commitments, for example relating to the speed or scope of Chinese liberalisation. In the latter case, where the Commission cannot point to a specific legal commitment that the Chinese have to follow, it is doubly important that we work very closely with you in the private sector, not least because you are often in the best position to convince the Chinese that what we are asking for is often in their own best interests.  So we will continue to state, firmly but fairly, the problems to the Chinese authorities, and to demand results. The results of this pressure have not been negligible, even during the short four months since my last visit to Beijing. Just as one or two examples:  in insurance, we are very pleased that some of the frankly excessive capital requirements for the opening of branches will be reduced, and we are currently looking at the substance of the new measures. But problems remain in this sector, such as the waiting period for a foreign company to be licensed in a second city, and we will continue to insist on full compliance with world_trade_organization commitments. And excessive capital requirements remain a problem in the financial services sector as a whole. in construction, we are generally pleased that foreign contractors who have the necessary qualification certificates can now apply for extension of this status, but we are still waiting for the phasing out of residence requirements for engineers and architects. on car financing, we were very pleased to see the long overdue opening in this area, where the Chinese were far behind schedule in implementing their world_trade_organization commitments, But I understand from european_union players in the market that we are still not where we expected to be and I therefore encourage the Chamber to follow developments in this sector closely. I am also very concerned that the Chinese appear to be going ahead with their idea of separate distribution channels in the car sector. This will be a major problem. and in the difficult area of sanitary and phytosanitary issues, we are pleased, since the announcement of the lifting of the ban on Dutch products when I was last in Beijing, that China has now recognised the United Kingdom as free of the disease of "foot and mouth". But more generally in this area, we remain very keen that China should implement international standards for example in the import of cosmetics and wood packaging. All that is on the positive side of the ledger. But we still have some very serious concerns, for example in the following areas:  there are still problems in the way in which China manages quotas and tariff rate quotas, particularly for cars and fertilisers. enforcement of intellectual property rights remains a big problem, particularly in the ambiguous criminal liability standards, the high thresholds required to prosecute intellectual_property_rights infringements, and the lack of enforcement at a local level. telecoms remains a problem, and the old problem of restrictions on the choice of a joint venture partner, which are not just contrary to world_trade_organization commitments undertaken by China, but which formally breach national treatment. And we have made clear to the Chinese authorities that we will insist that the granting of licences, notably in the area of 3G mobile is made in a transparent, objective and discriminatory manner. A lot of progress, by the way, can be made at the government to government level by establishing regulatory dialogues, which enable united_states to address many of the non-tariff barriers which reduce the actual benefits of liberalisation. For example, the Regulatory Dialogue, the Industrial Policy Dialogue, and of course the Information Society Dialogue are all good examples of this. And we are more than ready to also raise, in a government to government context, broader issues with a trade policy dimension, such as the Working Group on energy.  Of course, all this work is very resource intensive for you in the private sector, as well as for the Commission, the Member States and the Chinese authorities themselves. But I cannot stress enough that we on the government side cannot do it without you. Your behind the scenes pressure is not just invaluable, it is essential. And you must continue your efforts to exercise it outside Beijing, given that implementation is most often a local question, as I have observed in visits to Shanghai and Guangdong.  For our part, apart from insisting on a very regular basis on all trade problems with the Chinese side directly, what are we doing? First, it is not our style to wave a big stick, but to help the Chinese financially with the improvements we are seeking. As I have told you before, we are spending 22 million euros at the moment on programmes to assist the Chinese on implementation, and this will be increased to €37 million from next year. Second, we are maintaining the pressure in Geneva, and particularly with the Transition Review Mechanism, for which the second annual programme is just getting under way. We continue to take this very seriously, and intend to follow up some of the answers given to united_states by the Chinese authorities last year, which did not always go as far as we would like.  european_union and China in the context of the Doha Development Agenda  Talking of Geneva brings me nicely to the latest situation in the world_trade_organization Round of multilateral trade negotiations. When I was here in June, hope still sprang eternal for the Fifth world_trade_organization Ministerial meeting in Cancun in September. But the hope has long since sprang out of Cancun. We all know what happened there, and the result is a major set-back for the Doha Development Agenda. I will not dwell on the implications here, but it is clear that is now that any one ready to put a lot of money on conclusion of the Round by the agreed timeframe of the end of 2004 may have more money than sense.  All world_trade_organization Members stand to lose. But if I may, I think that China is one of the principal losers who stood to gain much from a positive outcome, including most obviously in market access. In other words, we do not regard China membership of the G20 as some expression of indifference to the multilateral trading system, as some declaration of war on the part of the south against the north. China has far too great a stake in a successful outcome of the doha_development_agenda for that, and I was delighted to have confirmation of that at the highest level from this visit. Indeed, when it comes to what China has to gain, just consider it is clear that China is in the enviable position of having everything to gain, and nothing to lose. For example, consider the fact that with an average tariff of 11% on textiles and clothing, China can only gain if the Round succeeds, and if there is thereby a substantial reduction in the average tariff of 29% that India puts on the same products, or in the 35% figure which applies for Brazil. And many of you will also know that this simple assessment doesnt even touch on peak tariffs in textiles, which makes the case even stronger.  So where next ? For our part, the european_union has made it clear that it would be a mistake to pretend that we can simply pick ourselves up, brush ourselves down, and to claim that we can move forward with business as usual as if nothing had happened in the interim. Cancun was a major failure. So we are in reflection and consultation mode within the european_union - we are reflecting inside the Commission, with the Member States, the Parliament, and with all our major constituencies inside the european_union, including of course business. In this consultation, we are asking rather fundamental questions, including the fundamental one of whether we in the European Union maintain our traditional preference for the multilateral system.  Here, I am happy to announce that early results of our consultation - with no lesser a body than the European Council, and similar echoes from the European Parliament - conclude that we not only reconfirm our multilateral approach, but we are open to an early resumption of the doha_development_agenda negotiations. The key phrase, however, is perhaps the stress on the commitment by all world_trade_organization members to make progress in the doha_development_agenda. As the European Council said, this will be indispensable to any successful resumption of the negotiations. So a major part of my visit to China this week, and of course a major part of my visit to New York and Washington next week, is to see what scope there is, what appetite there is, for an early re-launch, beyond the usual diplomatic formulas that we read every day on the news wires.  Although China is clearly keeping a boot in both bilateral and multilateral camps, I have had very strong confirmation that China wants to maintain a very good level of multilateral co-operation. Indeed I am very pleased to announce that we will shortly launch a new high level dialogue on trade and world_trade_organization issues between the Vice Minister of MOFCOM and our own Director General for Trade, Peter Carl. We are very keen that China retains her current position at the centre of world_trade_organization gravity, a constructive and reliable partner who does not go hunting for support amongst the extremes.  So in conclusion, as I leave China, I retain a fundamental sense of optimism as to China determination to build both a strong relationship with the european_union, and a strong multilateral system. Whatever view we take of the future development of the world economy and indeed international relations, there are very few models which suggest that european_union - China relations will not be pivotal to the century which we have still only just begun. I strongly believe that trade will be one of the key elements of that, and I feel sure that we will look back at this period as a vital moment in securing the long term future of the european_union-China relationship.

title: Pascal Lamy EU Trade Commissioner EU and China Strategic Partners Address at the EU-China Investment and Trade Forum Brussels, 6 May 2004
text: Today we are honoured and pleased to receive a large Chinese delegation at the highest level. I would like to extend my thanks to the Europe-China Business Association which has organized this important meeting. It is indeed a historic meeting, because it is the first top level trade delegation to come to Brussels since China accession to the world_trade_organization. It is an honour to receive in Brussels Prime Minister Wen, and I am of course also pleased to see my colleague, the new Chinese Minister of Commerce, Mr Bo Xilai, here in Europe so soon after taking up his post.
