I am also happy to see large presence from the European business community. You have seized the opportunities offered by the giant transformation underway in the Chinese economy. Contracted investments by european_union companies in China increased by 30% last year which testifies to the attention you pay to this market, to the point that China is now already the european_union second largest trade partner.  China and the european_union are both changing, and this should be mirrored in our trade and economic relationship. China has developed and continues to grow at breakneck speed. The european_union has just finalised a truly historic enlargement. During my recent visit to China in mid-March, in a speech at the european_union Chamber of Commerce in Shangai, I highlighted a concept which is central to the european_union integration, namely the concept of "quality", expressing the hope that european_union and China would be able to build a "quality relationship". What do I mean by that?  This means that, beyond the figures of the growth of our bilateral trade, it is indeed the quality and the content of our bilateral trade and economic relationship that in the future need to attract most of our attention and of our efforts. I think this mirrors well the concerns expressed by the Chinese leadership that what is essential for a sustainable economic development is more the content of the growth than the absolute figures as such. And the question of quality in european_union-China relations is of course equally relevant on questions of politics, science or culture. But I shall leave that to others.  As far as trade is concerned, cooperating more closely in the current world_trade_organization round of trade negotiations, the Doha Development Agenda is the key element towards building this quality relationship. Closely behind comes China progress with regards to world_trade_organization compliance, which I have of course both discussed in detail with Minister Bo.  But why do I insist on the doha_development_agenda? The answer is two-fold. First, because it is a multilateral negotiation, a format which the european_union is inherently comfortable with, and I hope China is becoming more comfortable with. Second, because it covers more than just market access. Trade will only remain a key driver of world prosperity if the rules are able to keep up with economic reality. Although we know that our Chinese friends have had to work hard to implement the world_trade_organization accession agreement, we hope China will, together with the european_union, take a constructive role commensurate with its trade and economic potential.  Indeed we are now intensifying our bilateral contacts on this issue, and today we will sign an agreement setting up a Trade Policy Dialogue between united_states. This Dialogue will be a useful instrument to develop our cooperation on world_trade_organization matters and also to address major bilateral issues, as well as trade in textiles.  Not only China is undergoing tremendous changes in these days, so is Europe. We have just completed european_union enlargement. It is hard to overestimate the importance of the final end to the division of Europe, in political and economic terms. What counts for you is that the European Union has been enriched with 10 new members, who have traditional and solid trade relations with China.  This friendly merger' will create the biggest single market in the world with over 450 million consumers, bigger than the united_states, Mexico and Canada combined, accounting for roughly 20% of world trade and contributing to more than a quarter of the world GDP. The accession of the ten new Member States will provide simpler access to the whole region. For example, a Chinese investor established in Slovenia will now have the freedom to distribute goods or provide services throughout the european_union and of course under the same conditions as investors from the existing european_union countries.  An important detail: The new Member States are already part of the landmark european_union-China tourism agreement which entered into force on 1 May 2004. Thanks to this agreement more Chinese tourists will be able to choose among the best of european_union tourist destinations, such as Brussels! Indeed, Minister Bo, you are perhaps a forerunner of the Chinese tourism explosion, which we very much welcome.  Not least because, the next step in strengthening our relationship will be not only the exchange of goods and services, but more and more direct contact between our peoples. Your presence here is part of this development, which is vital for a true European-Chinese partnership. And of course, I wish this conference the best possible success.  Thank you for your attention

title: Vice-President of the European Commission, Commissioner for Transport and Energy Energy issues in the European Union and the importance of promoting China-EU energy cooperation EU-China Energy Conference on EU-China Energy relations
text: Energy issues in the European Union and the importance of promoting China-european_union energy cooperation  european_union-China Energy Conference on european_union-China Energy relations Brussels, 25 May 2004  Vice Minister, Excellencies, Ladies and Gentlemen,  It is a pleasure to be here today at the 5th european_union- China Energy Conference. Allow me to start by quoting an old Chinese proverb: "Sail when there is a favourable wind." I think there has never been a period where there was a more favourable wind in the relations between the People Republic of China and the European Union. The recent visit to Europe of the Chinese Prime Minister Wen Jiabao is the best proof of the excellent partnership that China and the european_union have established over the last few years.  The intensified cooperation has also been crowned by success in my area of responsibility. In October 2003, a strategic partnership with China was inaugurated by the signing of the Galileo Cooperation Agreement. The fact that China was the very first third country, with which such an agreement was concluded, is a clear demonstration of the importance that we attach to cooperation with China. Another milestone in the bilateral transport relations was the conclusion of a comprehensive european_union-China Maritime Agreement in 2003. A first exchange of views has also recently taken place in the sector of aviation, with a view to a further intensification of the bilateral relations.  The state of play of cooperation and the possibilities of a further deepening of our relations in the other sector under my responsibility, the area of energy, is the subject of this Conference, during which representatives of the European and Chinese industry will discuss together with high-ranking officials from the european_union and China. As an event jointly organised by the Commission Directorate General for Energy and Transport and the Chinese Ministry of Science and Technology, I believe that it is an appropriate forum to address issues of common concern for both the european_union and China in the field of energy.  1. european_union-China energy relations  When discussing our relationship, it is difficult not to talk about China overall economic progress, which has been doubtlessly one of the most important conditioning factors. Today China total GDP is already the 6th largest in the world, and China economy in terms of purchasing power ranks already second in the world. The fast growing Chinese economy combined with a continuing population growth is expected to lead to a proportionally rapid growth in domestic demand for energy products, particularly oil. I will come back to this later on.  Ladies and Gentlemen,  Our partnership is certainly not a new one. The european_union and China have been co-operating in the field of energy for two decades.  In addition, since 1994, policy dialogue has taken place in the context of the Conference on european_union-China Energy Co-operation gathering together high-level European and Chinese representatives from industry and the administration every two years. The previous Conference, which I also had the pleasure to attend, took place in June 2001 in Beijing. I am convinced that this, the 5th Conference, will be equally successful.  Both China and the european_union face similar problems, notably the security of energy supply for the next decades and sustainable energy development. The development of environmentally friendly technologies in the coal sector is another important issue, which needs to be tackled in the near future. We have already considerable experience in the use of renewable energies, which we would like to share with you.  The european_union is prepared to support you in meeting the abovementioned challenges and to mutually exchange our experiences. I suggest that our future cooperation should also include discussions on the areas of gas and oil and on energy forecasts in general. As regards nuclear energy, we would be pleased if the proposed agreement on the peaceful uses of nuclear energy could be concluded this year.  This Conference aspires to be yet another step towards the intensification of relations in the energy field. I am confident that the participation of representatives from the European and Chinese energy sectors will ensure a fruitful discussion on all the abovementioned topics of common interest and will contribute in overcoming the occurring obstacles.  Allow me now to enter into a little more detail as regards the specific topics the Conference is going to cover:  2. Energy Policy and Security of Supply  Security of energy supply is the cornerstone of our energy policy. There are two aspects to this. First, there is a growing demand for energy products not only in Europe but also world-wide and - not least - in China. Second, our economies are increasingly dependent on supplies from external resources. For instance, the european_union is the largest net energy-importing region in the world and european_union energy dependence is expected to grow from 50% at present to 70% in 2030 unless decisive action is taken.  Taking into account this reality, the European Union has developed a new approach to energy policy, which recognises the clear link between energy supply and energy demand. This approach puts, for the first time, energy demand management at the forefront of energy policy. It emphasises diversification in energy supply, including the promotion of renewables and a fresh look at the nuclear option. It also stresses the importance of a permanent dialogue between energy Producers and Consumers.  In the coming years our economies will be increasingly dependent on oil. Fluctuations in oil prices can have a distorting and destabilising effect on the economies of both producers and consumers. The lack of certainty on security of supply and the opacity of the markets, which give rise to speculation, are the two factors that account for high price levels. This situation works mainly to the detriment of energy importers. Given that energy markets are globalised, energy importers find themselves in a situation where they have to compete for limited energy supplies on world markets, thus, creating tension among them. This is a potentially risky situation, which requires strategic management.  According to the Green Paper, more than 70% of the world oil reserves are located in the member countries of OPEC. Both the european_union and China will become increasingly dependent on the Middle East. If no specific measures are taken to disengage the oil sector, especially in transport, the european_union oil dependence could reach 90% by the year 2020. In 2003, China became the second biggest importer of oil. The rise in the Chinese demand for oil was seriously underestimated as it reached the levels that according to forecasts China was expected to reach by the year 2010.  Consequently, it is obvious that the european_union and China to a large extent share the same concerns on future oil supply. Therefore, closer and more transparent relations between united_states are indispensable.  We have also to work together so that a permanent and efficient Producer-Consumer dialogue be in place. This has been one of the priorities of the Commission but we must recognise that so far this dialogue has not achieved concrete results. That is why the Commission has developed a set of bilateral dialogues with key producing countries such as Russia, Algeria or Iran.  In addition to the development of an efficient Producer-Consumer Dialogue, we need to take measures in order to manage our energy dependence. As I said, we in the european_union believe that our efforts should be focused notably on energy demand and diversification of energy sources. The Commission is ready to give high priority on co-operation in these fields.  Measures to decouple global oil demand from growth will also have clear benefits. Fuel efficiency throughout the transport system will save united_states time and reduce pressure on the oil market. Alternative fuels (bio-fuels, natural gas, and hydrogen) will have to be introduced during the coming 10-20 years. In this context, I welcome the idea of holding an international conference on the use of hydrogen that takes place on this very day in Beijing.  3. The evolution of energy markets  Europe has been developing a model of energy markets based on regulated competition. While security of supply and public service are key underlying concerns, competition will lead to greater efficiency and benefits to consumers.  The enlarged european_union with 25 Member States encompasses a population of some 450 million and has thus become the world largest single energy market. The energy industry is a huge part of the European economy, with a turnover of € 250 billion per year. It is a vital component of industrial competitiveness. Our objective is to create a market, which functions effectively in the long term, including not just the current now 25 Member States but also European Union close neighbours, notably Russia.  I know that you are very interested in our experience of opening our energy market in Europe. Let me say a few words about where we stand in this field.  The european_union agreed in the summer of 2003 a package of legislation to complete the creation of its energy internal market. Major changes in the organisation of electricity and gas markets have already been made. In the next few years we will see the completion of a process, by which the industry will pass from a situation where monopolistic suppliers served closed national and regional markets to the creation of an operational internal market. A market is operational if there is competition at the level of both production of electricity and gas, and downstream. In this new market consumers will be able to choose their supplier from a range of companies active in the european_union market.  In July 2004, all commercial customers will be able to choose freely their suppliers throughout the Union. In July 2007, all consumers including households will have this right; the market will then be fully open to competition. Two thirds of the market for electricity and 80% of that for gas are today open to competition on a European scale. For both gas and electricity, customers are clearly beginning to use their new rights to shop around for new suppliers and put former monopolies under competitive pressures.  These developments will bring further important benefits for consumers, which will benefit from competitive energy prices. But also on the supply side, there will be new opportunities for investors wanting to enter the newly created competitive market.  4. Sustainable energies, energy efficiency and demand management  Let me turn now to the issue of energy and the environment. One important issue here, which we would like to address in this Conference, is the development of environmentally friendly technologies in the coal sector.  The future of coal depends largely on the development of techniques to reduce the environmental impact of the use of coal. Cleaner combustion technologies and CO2 sequestration are important aspects to be taken into consideration. China is the world largest producer and consumer of coal. Coal covers some 70% of China primary energy needs and represents nearly 90% of the fuel used in the electricity sector. This is therefore a key area for further deepening our co-operation. I see this Conference as a great opportunity to discuss the transfer of know-how on the use of clean coal technologies.  I have already mentioned the importance of energy efficiency when it comes to security of supply. However, energy efficiency also plays a key role in our policies to reduce the environmental impact of the energy sector. The european_union has recently adopted key measures in this field, namely the directive on promotion of cogeneration and the directive on energy performance of buildings. When fully implemented, these directives should make another valid contribution to further exploiting the energy saving potential, which exists in these sectors.  The use of renewable energy has increased considerably in the european_union in the last few years. New legislation has been put in place, for example on bio fuels, and Member States and the european_union have set clear targets to be achieved in this important area. Both energy efficiency and renewable energy are key aspects of our policies to combat climate change and to meet Kyoto commitments.  We cannot claim victory yet, but much progress has been made. However, better regulation will not produce results unless it meets with a positive response from operators, therefore the legislation must be backed up by wide dissemination of the best examples of technological development.  Energy efficiency and renewable energy should also feature prominently in our co-operation with China. Much has already been achieved. The first example of this is a € 20 million energy/environment co-operation programme, initiated and discussed in the working group on energy, and adopted by the Commission in December 2001. The objective of this programme is to ensure a sustainable use of energy through the improvement of energy efficiency, the development of renewable energies and the increased use of natural gas.  Moreover, a training programme on the use of the Kyoto Protocol flexibility mechanisms was launched in early 2003, and a training programme on the development of renewable energies in the context of the Kyoto Protocol was started at the end of 2003. Last but not least, the efforts to increase China participation in the 5th european_union Research Framework Programme have led to some success; 18 projects have started, out of which more than 50% deal with energy-related subjects.  Conclusion  Ladies and Gentlemen,  There is no doubt that energy must be at the forefront of co-operation between China and the european_union and play a key role in establishing a strategic partnership. For this, we need your contribution and through your cooperation I am confident that this relationship will render considerable benefits to both sides. We both face common challenges: security of energy supply, open markets and climate change. We have to work together, and we look very much forward in doing so. Let this event mark a milestone in this process.  Thank you, Ladies and Gentlemen, for your attention.

title: EU-China Summit: new steps in a growing relationship
text: european_union-China Summit: new steps in a growing relationship At the 7th Annual european_union-China Summit, which takes place on 8 December in The Hague, the European Union and China will further strengthen their maturing strategic partnership. The european_union will raise topics including the fight against illegal migration, and human rights, and discussions will also focus on economic issues including the Doha Development Round, Textiles and other trade concerns. A number of international issues including North Korea and Burma/ Myanmar will also feature. A joint declaration will be signed on non-proliferation and arms control and agreements will also be concluded on customs co-operation, and science and technology. It is hoped that agreement can also be reached on joint research on the peaceful use of nuclear energy. The European Commission will be represented by President José Manuel Barroso, Commissioner for External Relations and European Neighbourhood Policy, Benita Ferrero-Waldner, and Commissioner for External Trade, Peter Mandelson. The Chinese delegation will be led by Prime Minister Wen Jiabao.  Prior to the Summit, Commission President José Manuel Barroso commented:  "We are at a dynamic moment in our relationship with China. Our Chinese partners have acknowledged the importance of the european_union as a strategic partner and our relationship is growing in the political as well as trade fields. Developing this relationship will be one of our top foreign policy objectives in the years to come".  The Summit will reflect the fact that the european_union dialogue with China now covers all the essential areas of a modern partnership, from security issues such as non-proliferation and the fight against terror, to trade and economic co-operation, science and technology and the environment, as well as sensitive questions like human rights. Given the breadth of our common agenda, the two sides will therefore explore the possibility of concluding a new european_union-China framework agreement in the coming year. A wide range of agreements will be signed at the summit. See MEMO/04/284: european_union-China Summit: Agreements to be signed on 8 December.  Main issues to be discussed at the summit include:  The european_union wish to begin negotiations as soon as possible for a Readmission agreement to cover illegal immigration The Chinese desire to see the european_union arms embargo lifted The prospects for improved co-operation on human rights China implementation of its world_trade_organization commitments, the international Doha Round of world_trade_organization negotiations, the abolition of textiles quotas end 2004, and new mechanisms for further economic and sectoral co-operation between the european_union and China China request to the european_union to be granted full market economy status. A Joint Working Group has recently held its first meeting to discuss progress in solving this technical issue. In 2004 - designated "the year of Europe in China" - the european_union has enjoyed an unprecedented number of contacts with China, including PM Wen visit to Brussels. Following the Enlargement on May 1st, the european_union has become China largest trading partner and China is now the european_union second largest commercial partner. 2004 has seen operations begin under the new Galileo Satellite Navigation agreement and the entry into force of the milestone "Tourism Agreement" (formally known as the Approved Destination Status Agreement) which will facilitate visits to Europe by Chinese tourists.  The range of nearly 20 european_union-China sectoral "dialogues" (from Intellectual Property Right Protection and industrial policy to civil aviation and environmental protection) will soon expand to include social security and employment, economic issues, and the regulation of financial markets.

title: José Manuel Barroso  President of the European Commission Speaking points  Press conference of the 7th EU-China Summit The Hague, 8 December
text: I am very happy that my first summit with China was indeed a very fruitful summit. Considerable progress was made in many areas since the last summit : agreement on tourism, satellite navigation for example.  Today, we were able to sign several important agreements which will increase our cooperation in the field of science and technology (including nuclear research), will strengthen the links between our customs administration and facilitate exchanges of students.  China is emerging as a global player across the board and developing this relationship will be one of our external policy objectives during my mandate. I believe it is in the interest of both the european_union and China to embark on this long-term strategic relationship.  The european_union has become China most important trade partner and our 2nd trade partner. This more than anything gives you an idea of the strength of our relationship.  I believe that this should lead united_states to a new european_union-China framework agreement very soon which will include a strong trade and investment component but also all the elements of our cooperation which is now very broad. This includes human rights and human and cultural exchanges.  I welcome the important measures and initiatives announced by Prime Minister Wen, which materialise this upgrading of the quality and intensity of our relations. On the Commission side, we are ready and eager to correspond accordingly setting up similar mechanisms of cooperation at high level.  A reinforced relationship also implies mutual responsibilities : there are important issues where we could and should jointly exercise leadership : the Doha development round is a point in case where China should play a role commensurate to its importance in world trade;  I also believe that China as a global player has a responsibility in making sure that the end of the textile quotas will not lead to an uncontrolled surge of Chinese exports, damaging for less developing countries. China has a role to play in avoiding this by applying a policy of moderation;  I accept the kind invitation of Prime Minister Wen to visit officially China.  I am personally committed to european_union-China relations and so is the European Commission. I am sure that during the next five years there will be great progress in the strategic partnership between the european_union and China.

title: Peter Mandelson  EU Commissioner for Trade The EU-China relationship The EU-China Business Summit
text: When President Barroso asked me to represent him in opening this Summit with the Prime Ministers and Minister Bo Xilai, I agreed with great pleasure. It is heartening to see European and Chinese companies gathered here. On President Barroso behalf, I extend a warm welcome to you all.  The clear message I want you to take away today is this: the new European Commission recognises the economic importance of China and will place it firmly and centrally on its radar screen. We must review and lift our relations with China to a new and higher, more intense level. We should ask - are the conditions ripe to establish a new more comprehensive, overarching framework for the european_union China relationship. Why do I say this?  We are polities at the extremities of the same land mass. Asia and Europe are no longer separate, far-away Continents. It is, if you like, a defining moment in history. Of course, it is a truism that the world has become smaller as a result of globalisation. But forget the rhetoric. Europeans have to sit up and take notice because in absolute and relative terms, China is a huge phenomenon to be reckoned with.  China is the great change of our time. This is to your credit, Prime Minister, and to that of your predecessor Zhu Ronghji and others. Since Deng Xiaoping opened the door to the outside world some twenty five years ago, per capita income in China has doubled, and then doubled again, and then doubled once more. Within that single generation more than three hundred million people have been lifted out of poverty. A peasant society is urbanising apace. Once one of the most inward-looking countries, China has become the world largest consumer of steel, copper and cement, and an export powerhouse as well.  China has been through no "Big Bang", no economic collapse, and no sudden, unplanned liberalisation and privatisation: so different from the Soviet Union and its former Empire, as China leaders have always pointed out. China leaders have pursued intelligent, step by step economic reforms , in which each change is built on the confidence earned from a successful earlier change.  There is an expression in Chinese that says that "you must cross the river by feeling the stones." That has been your recipe for success: pragmatism combined with a determined sense of purpose and direction.  In the past 25 years China foreign trade has increased by an average 15% a year. It will pass the 1 trillion dollars threshold for the first time in 2004.  Europeans must ask themselves profound questions about what this means for them, the world economy, global institutions and the way they are run, and the challenges facing humanity. How can we anticipate the possible expansion of China by a further factor of four in the next 25 years?  My first point is that we must act together as Europeans. The European Union should be the central vehicle for a new policy of higher level engagement. Member States, even our largest, are too small to address the challenges posed by China on their own. A more intensive political interaction can pave the way for the economic exchange that will inevitably bind united_states closer together. Interdependence will grow: politically we must decide how we wish to depend on each other, and what rules should apply.  Second, the resurgence of China should not stir up fear of China. Make the mistake of treating someone as a threat and it becomes a self fulfilling prophecy. In fact I believe quite the opposite. We are not fundamentally that different. We are facing many of the same challenges. Both of our peoples live in densely populated parts of the world. Nature has given united_states relatively less agricultural land than other countries. We both have relatively less energy resources too. For different reasons our populations will face the same huge demographic challenge after 2015, with a smaller base of working people relative to retired citizens.  Europeans and Chinese share similar work ethics and an attachment to a solid social fabric. Although you are less wealthy today than we are, you wish a society where talents flourish but where individuals show responsibility to each other. So do we.  I have been told that the Dutch in their zeal to trade are sometimes referred to as the Chinese of Europe. As a Brit I am tempted to reply, perhaps that is true, but the greatest days of the Dutch in Europe were in the seventeenth century before the Brits came along. Be that as it may, you in China have become traders like the Dutch and English, and we admire your industriousness.  But let not forget that in earlier times the capacity to trade depended only too often on the capacity to put cannons on boats and finance marauding missions. This has changed thanks not least to the establishment of a rules-base trading system. world_trade_organization is creating the stability, predictability and confidence in the trading system that cannons could never inspire.  In today world, I am suggesting that we should make more room at the table for China. But as business people, you know that there is no such thing as a free lunch. With privilege comes responsibility.  In the economic field, China has pursued very courageous reforms and is making strides towards becoming a full market economy. Europe is looking to China today for further change, which is politically possible, precisely because you have developed a stable and competitive foundation of trading success. You have built the world second largest foreign exchange reserves, now over $500 billion, which are a solid guarantee of stability and confidence. This makes it possible for you to contemplate a step by step liberalisation of your financial and exchange rate policy, so that they become more responsive to market forces. Reforms I know are underway, but I would urge you to build on these as rapidly as you can.  A second overarching responsibility of China is to the world_trade_organization. As a member since 2001, it is now your organisation; indeed you are one of its four or five most important members.  The world_trade_organization depends on you to flourish. Some doubt China commitment to the Doha negotiations, on the grounds that you have already made your world_trade_organization contribution through your accession. Prime Minister, may I suggest that the success of the Doha Development Agenda is in your interest. We all need a balanced package on agriculture, tariffs, services, intellectual property, and tighter use of anti-dumping measures.  We must devote equal effort in Brussels and Beijing to press others to bring the doha_development_agenda to a successful conclusion. The smaller, more vulnerable countries at the outer edges of world trade need the doha_development_agenda benefits as life support. And both of united_states need the world_trade_organization to grow in strength because we have a common interest in developing strong and impartial international organisations.  At the end of this month China reaps its first huge dividend from world_trade_organization membership, with the final removal of textile quotas: a remarkable triumph for the world_trade_organization system given the upheaval that the final fulfilment of this longstanding commitment is likely to cause. This liberalisation is a major prize for you. But I would urge you to be sensible about the growth of your exports - not to stop you exporting a lot more, but to moderate the growth of those exports - over a reasonable time period. Not for the sake of domestic protection in Europe, but in recognition that in countries, for example, like Bangladesh, Sri Lanka and Mauritius, the fate of millions of workers hangs by some fraying textile threads. They face the prospect of harsh upheaval, grim poverty and possibly civil unrest if they are not given time to adjust to the new competition from China.  This is not the time to go through a checklist of european_union-China current trade issues. We discuss these in a frank and constructive spirit, and I want to thank personally Minister Bo for the efforts to solve the export restrictions on Chinese coke earlier in the year.  Standing in for my President, I will also want to make a few remarks about our political relationship. In my view, and together with my colleague Mrs. Ferrero-Waldner, I believe that our response to future social and environmental and even geopolitical challenges is likely to be comparable. Our interests in many areas, like global warming, in the long run coincide. In our foreign policy we both privilege the use of soft power instruments.  We both share an interest in being partners not rivals with the most powerful nation in the world - the United States. We look to China to play a greater role from your country in international peace-keeping. The world needs the enormous contribution you can make.  Also, both in Europe and China we can improve our protection of human rights. So we should vigorously pursue our dialogue on this, based on mutual respect, confidence, and a wish to improve ourselves. As a one-time television journalist, before I exchanged this vocation for the calm waters of professional politics, I know the value of an open media for a vibrant, mature society.  The phenomenal success of your economic reforms should now have created the foundation and confidence for bolder moves. Internally for more political reform. Externally for stronger regional cooperation in North East Asia. In post war Europe, economics was the motor of European integration, but the driver was a genuine desire for post war reconciliation. In this, I hope Europe recent history still has something to teach your region.  So I come back to where I started: the need for a stronger framework for the european_union China relationship. Our current agreement dates from 1985. It does not tackle barriers that remain between our economies and which fall outside the remit of the world_trade_organization, nor does it provide a framework for overseeing other issues of mutual interest, such as migration. Would we not both benefit from a strong overarching framework that enables united_states to work further on new areas like readmission agreements, energy and transport, science and technology?  On the trade side this could imply going beyond the world_trade_organization in such areas like investment or government procurement. During your visit to Brussels in May this year, you Prime Minister Wen have said that intellectual property rights were in the minds of the Chinese as well. And we want to engage you on choices you are making for your telecommunications market, like the next generation 3G standard.  I have a sense that business would certainly be interested in the opportunities that a new Agreement could bring. At this juncture, I would like to express a word of thanks to the Dutch Presidency and Dutch business who have so ably organised the event in conjunction with the Europe-China Business Summit.  In conclusion, Prime Ministers, Minister Bo, I am honoured to have been able to address you. I hope that in the years ahead we will see real substance develop in an ever deeper relationship. If that is your wish, you can count on my every endeavour as Europe trade Commissioner to deliver upon it.  Thank you.

title: European Commissioner for Trade  The EU and China: Partnership and Responsibility in the Global Economy University of International Business and Economics, Beijing
text: The european_union and China: Partnership and Responsibility in the Global Economy  In this speech to the University of International Business and Economics in Beijing European Commissioner for Trade Peter Mandelson argues:  - That "there is no greater challenge for Europe than to understand China and to forge new ties of partnership" with it.  - That China new trading power carries with it new responsibilities. China has benefited from world_trade_organization membership and now has a key leadership role in a successful Doha round. China doha_development_agenda contribution will be crucial to a successful round.  - That China can show its commitment to the world_trade_organization system by, among other things, acting to moderate the rate of growth of textiles exports to avoid a sudden surge, and by improving the enforcement of it legislation protecting intellectual property rights.  - That "if there is a perception of a lack of equilibrium in China trading practice, a negative reaction will be inevitable."  This is my first visit to China in my job as Europe Trade Commissioner. But it is not my first visit to China. I have taken a close interest in the remarkable development of this country and in the broader phenomenon of Asian economic growth for many years.  I thought it was important to come here early in my mandate, because I see no greater challenge for Europe than to understand the dramatic rise of China and to forge new ties of partnership with your country.  I believe China has a similar interest in partnership with Europe. The european_union is also a remarkable and relatively new phenomenon: a continent of nations uniting together in the pursuit of prosperity, security and justice, both at home and abroad. Inside our borders the nation states of Europe have come together to create a single economic space, to build a body of shared law covering social, environmental and security policy, to adopt a single currency. Most recently we have enlarged and now include 10 new countries, many of them former satellites of the Soviet Union. The European Union is spreading wealth, democracy and stability across our continent. It is a dynamic force for change and for improving people lives.  On 9 December I spoke at the european_union China Business Summit in The Hague of the need to review and lift our relationship with China to a higher and more intense level. I believe in this goal. Today I want to examine how we can move this forward.  China is the great change of our time. In 25 years, you have doubled per capita income, and then doubled it again and then doubled it again. You have lifted three hundred million people out of poverty. China has become an export power house. Your exports to Europe have increased by an average 20% per year since 1983. Only last week, the Washington-based Earth Policy Institute announced that China has replaced the United States as the world top consumer of four out of five basic commodities: grain, meat, coal and steel. Only in the consumption of oil does the united_states still surpass China. Further upmarket the number of personal computers sold in this country doubles every two years.  As the new China defines itself economically and politically, so must the rest of the world define how to situate itself towards China. For many the instinct is not to trust that which you know too little about.  With some I have registered a sense of alarm or even a hint of fear of China rise. I have seen it in Europe and heard it in meetings with leaders in other parts of the world. And this worries me.  The reaction I want to promote is more positive and interactive. We need to understand China better, to learn how to work with her, and to benefit from this country growth. We must establish the conditions for a more mature and confident partnership.  We need to build this partnership because we are facing the same uncertainties. If globalisation teaches united_states one lesson it is surely that we need to address its darker sides collectively. They include terrorism, proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, regional conflicts, poverty, migration and international crime, epidemics, the shortage of resources and the threat of climate change.  All these problems affect China and they affect Europe, although not always in the same way. And China, as it grows, will become a more significant factor in the future, both in contributing to some of these problems and I hope in leading efforts to solve them.  I genuinely believe that, whatever their concerns, the majority of people in Europe are convinced that a stronger, co-operative relationship with China is not only inevitable but also very much in our own interest.  In my own area of responsibility - trade - the Chinese market offers a huge opportunity for European companies. China is a voracious consumer of European goods, technology and services. China is also a competitor in many areas - but competition, provided it is fair competition, is a good thing.  We in Europe are conscious that we need to step up our own economic performance and flexibility if we are to compete successfully. The new European Commission, of which I am a Member, has set this as its central task. Our mission is to stimulate greater dynamism in the European economy to promote growth and jobs, and through this to ensure that we can compete in global markets so that Europe high standard of living and social protection is preserved.  But here is the caveat. If we are to build a stronger partnership, I believe Europeans need to have a clearer sense that China is assuming the responsibilities that accompany its new power within the global community. As we all learn in life, the greater our power the greater our responsibility.  Europeans are now looking to China to play a greater role in the international community in many areas. For example in supporting the peace-keeping operations of the United Nations and in joining the collective approach to meeting the challenges of climate change.  In my own area of work, trade, I too am calling on China to demonstrate responsibility and leadership. In trade policy the greatest single challenge before united_states is to bring to a successful conclusion the ongoing Doha Development Agenda negotiations. China has an overwhelming interest in strengthening a rules based trading system. Those rules are already working in China favour and they will continue to do so in the years ahead. In the Doha Round we need to deliver a better global environment for trade which will reinforce the world_trade_organization and its rules of opportunity across the globe.  I am aware that many in China feel that this country paid a high price in 2001 to get into the world_trade_organization. I have been told that those negotiations were long and tortuous. They lasted fourteen years. Many Ministers, including the formidable Madame Wu Yi who I look forward to meeting tomorrow, were not able to conclude them. And your former Prime Minister Zhu Rongji is known to have said that the hairs of all of China world_trade_organization negotiators had turned from black to white by the time the talks were over. So I have to recognise that it was not an easy task.  Having said this, I do wish to say today that your membership of the world_trade_organization was neither the end, nor the beginning of the end. It was the end of a beginning which has already brought you many advantages as foreign investment keeps pouring in and your economy continues to surge forward. So although I recognise the particular status of China as a "recently acceded" world_trade_organization Member, we are looking to you to contribute again.  To put the Doha Round back in motion we need to move urgently to construct a balanced package covering agriculture, trade in industrial goods, and trade in services. And alongside this we need to strengthen the rules governing intellectual property and the use of anti-dumping measures.  I am looking to China, as the greatest, most powerful and most rapidly advancing developing economy, to show leadership in the run up to our Ministerial meeting in Hong Kong in December this year. I shall be engaging in detailed discussions with Chinese negotiators in the months ahead, and in particular in preparing the mini Ministerial meeting to be held here in July. In political terms, my few months in the job have already suggested to me that the doha_development_agenda will not succeed unless many developing countries are given the security that China will not take the lion share of the round benefits. In my view the only way to lay these concerns to rest may be for you to contribute more than may at first sight appear necessary or reasonable. I should add that this is often the price for leadership.  More immediately, China can demonstrate its commitment to the world trade system through its own behaviour within it. Take for example the textiles sector. Six weeks ago China reaped a remarkable down-payment from world_trade_organization membership, with the removal of textile quotas. This liberalisation is a major prize for you.  Already its effects are beginning to be felt by textile industries in Europe, and more importantly by textile industries in poorer developing countries around the world.  We have discussed this already at length. I have been encouraged by the readiness of China to consider introducing measures which may help to inhibit a dramatic surge of exports which could destabilise the new conditions under which world trade in textile products takes place.  But there is much concern that these measures will not be sufficient. Speaking for China major trading partner, I say to you openly as a friend, that it is important for China to measure the impact that your own success is having and will have on others in the world. I want others to welcome China success, to benefit from it. We can all benefit from liberalisation and we are not going back to the system of restrictions that existed for so many years. But I think that some moderation and caution in the rate of growth of Chinese textiles exports - pacing it over time - will help to ensure a harmonious adjustment to this new global trading environment.  Another key to avoiding negative reactions in Europe and elsewhere will be to ensure that access to China own booming market is open to others.  China has made remarkable progress in bringing its legislation on intellectual property up to date. But in many ways that is only the beginning. The real test is when you start to enforce intellectual property rights vigorously throughout the country. I wish to work with China urgently on this, to promote better enforcement. It is in your interest too, because you are fast becoming an innovation-based society and you want to attract more foreign technology. You cannot have the one without the other.  Other areas we need to work on together include liberalising the banking sector, strengthening accountancy standards and property rights more generally, and corporate governance. In some areas industrial policies continue to give undue privileges to domestic companies.  These are all key issues both for China and for her trading partners. Reform and opening will be progressive but it must happen. If there is a perception of a lack of equilibrium in China trading practices, a negative reaction will be inevitable. We in Europe have learnt that trade and development expand not only when we reduce tariffs and open borders, but when there is a convergence of rules and institutional frameworks governing economic activity. That is why we also want a deeper dialogue with China as you develop your regulatory system.  Of course China has other international responsibilities too, for example the gradual liberalisation of the exchange rate of the Yuan. I would welcome it if, as a first step, China currency can soon be linked to a broader set of currencies than the dollar, in order to reflect better its trading patterns. Further reform beyond this will be essential for the perception that fair trading conditions exist, although I am of course aware of the importance for China of preserving financial stability.  Another key area will be China impact on global markets for energy and natural resources. We need to create a framework that recognises interdependence and connects demand and supply.  And of course for Europeans the protection of human rights is a key concern. We should vigorously pursue our dialogue on this, in an atmosphere of mutual respect and confidence, and a desire always to improve our performance.  What emerges from all this, to my mind, is a rich agenda for Europe and China to work on in order to give substance to a new strategic partnership. This agenda spans both cooperation in international organisations and groups - like the world_trade_organization - and a bilateral agenda which we need to strengthen. We need a new initiative to galvanise this work.  As I said in The Hague, I believe we need a stronger contractual framework for our relationship, bringing together different areas of activity in a single over-arching agenda and vision. Our current bilateral agreement dates back 20 years. It belongs to the last century, not to this one. It does not allow united_states to address several vital economic issues between united_states, such as investment and government procurement, nor a broader agenda on areas such as readmission agreements, energy and science and technology. It does not recognize China phenomenal evolution.  A modern Europe and a modern China need a modern basis for their relationship - permitting broad, open and honest debate and negotiations. I accept that there has to be give and take on both sides. We in Europe are preparing to move forward to lift the arms embargo. But if and when we do, we shall find the step easier if we can point to clear evidence that China is taking account of our concerns in other areas of policy which in turn will allow united_states in Europe to feel confident about our next move.  The same goes for market economy status. I know this is of great political importance for China. And I understand why. I am hearing you.  But this is not exclusively a political issue. Recognition of market economy status has to be based on objective progress. We have set out what China in our opinion needs to do in order to meet those criteria. And I intend to submit more specific proposals in the very near future. Many of the requirements are in areas I have touched on today, affecting economic openness and governance.  I would like to be able to move forward on this. I am ready to discuss and exchange views on outstanding issues. We can work on this together.  But while the technical aspects are vital, I also believe that attitudes in Europe and elsewhere towards granting market economy status will be influenced by a broader perception of how China is developing, in its political, social and economic behaviour, both at home and abroad.  In a partnership between two entities as powerful and as complex as China and the european_union, progress in one field will inevitably affect progress in others. It has to be a give-give relationship, based on a prudent understanding of the concerns and requirements of the other partner, as well as a healthy and natural respect on each side for the other interests.  That is the sort of mature and complex political relationship that I believe Europe and China need to develop. It will require much more intense contact and greater mutual knowledge and confidence in the years before united_states. The sooner we move ahead the better. I want to contribute and play my part in this.

title: Peter Mandelson  EU Trade Commissioner EU-China negotiations on textiles Statement to the press
text: Statement to the press following european_union-China negotiations on textiles  Statement to the press Shanghai, China, 10 June 2005  From the beginning of this year the issue of Chinese textiles has been very firmly on my radar screen. This is not surprising. The explosion in imports of Chinese textiles and clothing has created alarm in many European member states, and the issue has threatened to mushroom into a major trade dispute between Europe and China.  Without wishing to turn back the clock on the elimination of quotas in the textiles trade I concluded that economically and politically I had to propose action to manage what was becoming a combustible situation in a number of our Member States. I am a believer in free trade. But I am equally sensitive to the need to be able to adjust and find transitional arrangements in the event of sudden developments and unexpected shocks.  I have always hoped that this issue could be resolved amicably, rather than through the european_union taking unilateral measures, even if we are legally entitled to do so. I know that not everyone shares this view. But my firm preference has been to seek an enduring agreement with our strategic partner China in view of our relationship and our mutual interest in strengthening our trade and investment ties over the long term.  I am therefore pleased that it has been possible for the European Commission and the government of China to find a mutually acceptable solution that will manage and ensure a smooth transition through a period extending over the next three years. The agreement provides for agreed transitional growth rates between now and the end of 2007, followed by a further year during which both sides will work together closely in the hope that trade can be conducted without further interference in this sector. These agreed rates will apply to the range of product categories that we have investigated. The categories represent the most sensitive for our Member States. They refer to almost all those mentioned in the request for action submitted by the European textile industry association Eurtex, and more.  Agreed growth rates will apply immediately and for the remainder of 2005. The rates will be adjusted upwards for 2006 and 2007 to provide for a smooth transition to full liberalisation during 2008. In principle the agreement should be a once-and-for-all overall agreement rather than a salami-slice approach that forces united_states to look at and haggle over every product on a category-by-category basis, as was being threatened without this agreement.  Today agreement will be fair on both sides. It provides clarity, certainty and predictability and will also provide relief for developing country textile exports to Europe. It is an agreement that helps everyone interests. It is a win-win-win agreement. The Commission has undertaken to exercise restraint concerning the application of the european_union rights under paragraph 242 of China world_trade_organization accession agreement as full liberalisation is resumed during 2008. I believe that the overall settlement represents a fair deal for China while giving respite and a much needed breathing space to European textile industry.  I want to say something more. Today agreement is a significant demonstration that China is entering the global economy as a responsible and valued partner. And I say this too. China is entitled to reap the comparative advantages and its world_trade_organization accession, while managing its integration into the global economy in a way that avoids fear of China, and in a way that does not provoke a protectionist backlash by European industry and the general public.  I would like to commend the way in which the Chinese government and Minister Bo Xilai in particular have responded to European concerns in this matter. I think this augurs very well for our future relationship.  Finally a quick word on next steps. Obviously this agreement will need to be endorsed by the european_union Member States, but I have already provided them with a first outline of the context of the agreement. I know that this is a difficult issue for many of them and I have been in close contact with several Member State Ministers and I trust that in their final assessment of the agreement from their different standpoints they will show a sense of european_union solidarity and unity and a desire to resolve this issue once and for all.

title: Peter Mandelson EU Trade Commissioner Trade policy for a ‘Social Europe'
text: My theme today is that progressive market opening and trade liberalisation are not hostile to the social and labour standards we seek internationally and in Europe. On the contrary, rising economic growth and wealth generation are the conditions we need for these standards to be achieved and sustained.  It is right for united_states to focus on this because in my judgement, the social dimension' is back.  The question of a Social Europe' will be at the heart of the debate about Europe future. Oui' or non', this conclusion will be widely drawn from Sunday referendum in France. And I have no doubt at all that social discontents and reform fatigue played a central part in last Sunday political earthquake in Germany.  In my remarks today, I want to reflect on what this renewed focus on the social dimension will mean - both for Europe in general, and my own responsibilities for trade globally.  Many of you attending this Forum may jump to the conclusion that as a Brit, I will find this renewed focus on the social dimension unwelcome. After all, arent the Brits, even pro-European Brits, only interested in Europe in order to drive forward enlargement and economic reform, strengthen the Single Market and promote free trade?  There may of course be the odd honourable exception like my good friend John Monks - but so truly exceptional is John, that it appears to underline the validity of the general rule!  Actually, I have always believed in the social dimension.  My preoccupation has not been to question its importance, but that the Social Europe we build should be modern and forward looking, rather than stuck in the past, defensive and protectionist. Its driving purpose should be to provide security by advancing opportunity rather than fruitlessly attempting to block change.  The need to accept the inevitability of change and reform is fundamental - and it has been brought home to me with exceptional force in my six months as Trade Commissioner.  Europe cannot stop the world and get off. We cannot pull the blankets over our heads and hide from the shifts taking place in the global economy.  Take China emergence as a world economic power. I pick on China not because I want to stoke fears. Rather the reverse. I want Europe to rise to the challenge of accommodating a new China as a constructive partner not a deadly rival.  China will continue to send its tremors but it is an emblem for what is happening on a wider scale in Asia and South America - and some day hopefully in Africa and the Middle East, as these markets and growing productive capacity start to emerge and participate in the benefits of globalisation.  Ten years ago, Chinese exports to Europe amounted to roughly a fifth of those of the United States. Today they amount to about three quarters. Over the last five years they have expanded at 23% a year - an estimated 38% in 2004.  For the past two months, most days of my life have been touched in one way or another by the problem of Chinese textiles. I have faced insistent demands for the imposition of safeguard measures against surges of imports on a scale that our domestic producers complain they cannot withstand or adjust to.  My point today is not whether you think I have done too little or too much. I have tried to strike a careful balance - acting objectively on the basis of the evidence put to me - yet robustly to demonstrate to the Chinese the seriousness of the problem of short term disruption on which I am urging them to act with united_states.  My point is a much more profound one - about the economic challenges ahead. Within our world_trade_organization obligations, safeguard measures are only available to deal with short term disruption. Permanent quotas cannot be re-imposed. The issues we face in textiles today will affect other sectors tomorrow. Intensifying competition exists in all the traditional industrial sectors: footwear, machine tools, consumer electronics, and cars. You name it: were going to experience it and the impact will be severe, not just in France and southern Europe where the volume of demands for action in textiles has been heard loudest, but across the european_union as a whole.  Of course the temptation is to cry foul. To denounce competition as unfair; to complain of artificial exchange rates; to protest that goods are being sold below long run sustainable costs; and to argue that wage levels reflect forced labour and the absence of trade union rights.  As the responsible political authorities in Europe, the Commission and the Member States have a duty to listen to these arguments, not dismiss them as a fantasy. We must, and will, be prepared to act where a well founded case can be made about unfair trading and we will pick up the appropriate instruments of defence.  But let united_states not deceive ourselves and not face realities. Europe is faced with a fundamental choice of directions.  One way is to take the line of least resistance and to bow to the populism either of the Left that rejects globalisation or the populism of the Right that blames foreigners for everything. But this is a cul-de-sac, because by putting up barriers between ourselves and world markets, we may save jobs in the short term but at the cost of our industries becoming globally uncompetitive in the longer term.  The only alternative is the difficult and painful tasks of reform and modernisation. It the policy that the Barroso Commission set out in February for Europe and its Member States, explaining our revised Lisbon strategy.  To prioritise Growth and Jobs is not a neo-liberal obsession. Yes, it about wide-ranging economic reform. Yes, it demands of united_states to press ahead with opening markets in Europe, in order to provide European business with a vibrant economic base on which it can compete in the rest of the world.  But it not a strategy to Americanise Europe. There are political choices open to united_states. Reform is for a purpose: to make our European model of society sustainable for generations to come. To allow united_states to protect the environment and to achieve the goal of sustainable development." Political choices can be made. Another world is possible".  I realise that for many trade unionists, the use of phrases like reform and modernisation' is seen as code for more labour market flexibility, less job security, a weakening of employment rights, and welfare reforms that reduce social benefits and/or make entitlement to them conditional'. And reform may indeed involve tough and unpalatable choices.  But the larger failure of economic reformers - and I accept the collective self-criticism - has been in not offering a strong enough vision of a new and appealing Social Model for Europe within which the short term pain can at least be understandable and justified, even when it still hurts.  My speech today is not the place to set out this concept in full because I want to broaden my remarks to you  Let me just give you a brief insight into my current thinking.  The Single Market was never conceived an end in itself. I still remember my feeling of excitement and pride when Jacques Delors re- launched the idea of Social Europe. His concept - that market liberalisation and the drive for competitiveness had to be matched by flanking measures to promote social cohesion and environmental sustainability is - still valid. The right social and environmental policies strengthen competitiveness and at the same time make reform more acceptable. But the achievements of Social Europe have in truth been too limited. True we have put in place a set of minimum social standards, which for all the furore they have aroused and still arouse, have improved millions of people lives, from the right to paid holidays to a comprehensive outlawing of all forms of discrimination at work. But beyond a minimum floor, Social Europe can make more progress.  In my view, the reason why Social Europe has been constrained is because it was seen as a bid for competence over social policy by the European institutions, rather than a shared endeavour with the Member States in a spirit of "unterritorial" partnership. Creating a modern Social Model for Europe is not a question of harmonising diverse social systems; it is instead a question of building on common values to face up to and tackle the consequences of economic change.  The challenge of today is to equip every citizen of Europe, from whatever social background, nationality, colour or religion, to fulfil their own individual potential in a rapidly changing world. The essence of our European cultural and religious tradition is this recognition of the uniqueness and equal worth of the individual. We then combine this essential insight of the Enlightenment with recognition of the need for a strong society, particularly in both the social catholic and social democratic traditions, to enable the individual to achieve fulfilment within a stable social framework.  The situation today is that these essential insights of our Europeanness remain valid. But the collective institutions and systems we built in the last century to underpin them have outlived their time - in particular, the social consensus corporatism, the social insurance welfare state and centralised universal public services that played such a crucial role in the era of mass industrial society.  The ends remain - but the means require modernisation and reform. What we need today are new approaches and new institutions to tackle the new social challenges of extending opportunity throughout the lifecycle- tackling inherited disadvantage by investing in the social support and education of young children and their mothers; providing high standards of schooling in ethnically diverse and socially fractured communities; promoting skills and lifelong learning for those who missed out at school; reaching for world class standards of excellence in higher education and research; opening access to retraining and help with adjustment for the victims of economic change; helping older workers reintegrate to the labour market and abolishing the traditional concept of retirement.  These are examples of the common challenges a modern Social Model should be addressing. And only when we have better defined them should we then address the second order question of the role of the European Union and its member States in tackling them successfully. I want the European Union collectively to find a way of addressing these issues in the months ahead.  Meanwhile if Europe must have a stronger social dimension, so should its trade policy.  From my perspective, trade has the potential to contribute to the overall objective of sustainable development in all its dimensions - economic, social, and environmental. Multilateral trade liberalisation can be a sound social policy. It can foster long-term economic growth, stimulate trade and investment and assist developing countries' integration into the world trade system. And it can do this in a way that will help combat poverty and raise living and working standards for all members of society.  Notice, however, my emphasis on the word can. There is no magic wand of free markets that guarantees it will. But I defy anyone in this room to set out an alternative strategy to a managed progressive market opening that will produce better results for developing countries.  The Social Dimension is not, of course, officially part of the world_trade_organization work. If we are honest, this is not surprising. Globalisation has integrated and dynamised market economies across the world. Its triumph has been breathtaking since the collapse of Communism. But globalisation has not created a world of shared values. I cannot tell whether the opposition of the developing world to inclusion of a meaningful Social Dimension will change. I hope in time it will. I suspect that the competitive issues arising from the rise of China will turn what was a North- South divide over trade-and-labour issues into a South- South debate.  I have never believed that a genuine concern for decent labour standards is covert protectionism. But when some people talk of the dangers of developing country imports as an "invasion", I have to say that it is not surprising that developing countries draw the opposite conclusion.  Globally, the European Union will continue to fly the flag for a meaningful social dimension. We had no hesitation in using our Trade Policy Review last autumn - an exercise in multilateral surveillance and benchmarking that every world_trade_organization Member undergoes - to demonstrate to others that trade and social policy should and can go hand in hand.  Our own trade policy instruments leave united_states more room to offer positive incentives. Our Generalised System of Preferences has for a number of years offered the possibility of additional concessions for countries that respect Core Labour Standards. We have just revised our GSP to put much more emphasis on sustainable development. Under the new GSP Plus, additional tariff preferences will be available to vulnerable countries which have both ratified and implemented the main international UN and ILO conventions on labour and human rights, environmental protection and good governance, including the fight against drugs.  And where countries systematically flout core labour standards, we will not hesitate to take away the positive trade incentives that we have put in place. Both international trade unions and the ILO have evidenced the systematic violations of freedom of association in Belarus.  As a result, I now intend to recommend to the Commission and our Member States that we proceed with the temporary withdrawal of GSP benefits for this country. If we are serious about labour standards, our incentives must be able to work both ways.  We also include a social dimension in our bilateral agreements. The Cotonou Agreement includes specific provisions on trade and labour standards, as will the Economic Partnership Agreements that replace it. Our agreements for example with South Africa and Chile cover social issues in a comprehensive way. For any future agreements, we will develop the mutual supportiveness between trade, environmental and social policies.  In addition we are committed to promote Corporate Social Responsibility, both on a European scale and globally with OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises.  To better understand the impact of our multilateral negotiations on social development (as well as economic and environmental aspects); we have undertaken sustainability impact assessments'. The SIA results are published, discussed openly and integrated in policy making. The dialogue is real.  But the assessments are not perfect: we lack expertise on how to measure the wider impact of trade policy, particularly on social policies. It is much easier to establish what trade policy should not do to labour standards and social issues, rather than what it can and should do.  These issues will come more to the fore as awareness of the condition of Asia rise, including China growth, spreads amongst the public. Separately, I have been challenged in the European Parliament on what I am doing as Trade Commissioner to protect the rain forest and come to the aid of other environmental victims. These social and environmental concerns need a considered response from united_states. They will not go away.  I intend therefore to build up our capacity both internally within the Commission, as well as our ability to support best practice overseas in policy making and institution building. We need more knowledge and skills in understanding the impact of trade policies on employment and working conditions - for example how to increase the employment intensity of growth, and how to assist adjustment to change through active labour market policies. Without better policies, the weakest and the poorest risk being left at the margins of globalization.  As a result, the Commission has launched a joint project with the ILO on devising methodology to measure the impact of trade liberalisation on domestic employment and social indicators. My aim is for this to become a general tool that our developing country trading partners can use in future to refine their trade policy. We ourselves stand to learn a good deal from this exercise.  I will also need to reflect on how to take forward our GSP+ and what new initiatives to develop. Our ongoing negotiations with our ACP partners on Economic Partnership Agreements will be a good opportunity to explore new ways of fostering sustainable development in regional trade agreements. In this, trade unions in the ACP countries have an important role to play and we should try our best to support their capacity to do so.  We should look for examples of best practice that others can emulate- for example, Cambodia. Despite being a Least Developed Country and all the problems that come with that, Cambodia has not only chosen a path of liberalisation and world_trade_organization membership but also made it clear that this should not come at the expense of social conditions.  Trade liberalisation and poverty eradication are going hand in hand offering a good model for others and contesting the view of some that these two things are incompatible. I believe that we have much to learn from this example, where a combination of local partnerships between unions, employers and the government and the support of the international community has lead to very positive results. The ILO of course plays a crucial facilitating role in this.  Also, the scope of our trade-related assistance should be widened beyond hard-core "trade" subjects like customs or sanitary control systems. Our joint project with the ILO should be seen as a first example of addressing the link between trade and adjustment in our assistance programmes.  There is a need for better co-ordination between the main international institutions - world_trade_organization, ILO, and Bretton Woods. We need more policy coherence and Europe will play its full part in promoting this.  In conclusion, let me repeat: I am as committed to advancing a modern social dimension in trade policy as I am to reframing a modern Social Model for Europe that will enable united_states to withstand today competitive challenge. I welcome debate and new ideas - which is why I am here today. It isnt easy. But nothing worthwhile is - and that why I will keep at this throughout my mandate as Trade Commissioner, and I look forward to working closely with the ILO in doing so.

title: José Manuel BARROSO President of the European Commission The EU and China: painting a brighter future together
text: Ladies and gentlemen,  It is a real pleasure to be with you today. This is my fourth visit to China but my first official visit as President of the European Commission. I have come here - on this special occasion of the 30th anniversary of our diplomatic relations - with one central message: the european_union is deeply committed to developing its strategic partnership with China further.  World peace, prosperity, progress, harmony, friendship, protection of the environment - these were key themes for hundreds of Chinese children when they took part in a painting contest held by the European Commission Delegation in Beijing to mark the thirtieth anniversary. The bright and colourful pictures clearly demonstrated the very high expectations our children have for our relationship and its benefits.  Have we come a long way together in these last thirty years? Yes we have. When Sir Christopher Soames, then European Commissioner for External Relations, arrived in China in 1975 to establish diplomatic relations, who could have imagined that, thirty years later, the european_union and China would be global players enjoying such a rich partnership? With total annual trade between united_states at €175 billion, the european_union is now China largest trading partner. And China is our second largest. But the relationship has now gone well beyond the commercial.  Our manifold achievements range from trade and tourism, to environment and energy. We now engage in twenty different dialogues in as many sectors. We have signed several major new agreements, including our milestone Approved Destination Status tourism and Galileo satellite navigation agreements, which have opened up Europe to thousands of Chinese tour groups and taken our scientific cooperation to a new level.  And the european_union remains a key contributor to China development and reforms: by providing access to our huge market; through inward investment of €10 billion per year; by fostering exchanges of expertise and technology through involvement in programmes such as the international thermonuclear fusion project; and last but by no means least through european_union financial cooperation, which is used to support China reform process.  Do we have difficulties? Of course we do, - as in any relationship we do not always see eye to eye on all issues. For example, only when there is a strong trade relationship are there trade problems. Nonetheless, we should be confident that our relations have matured sufficiently to deal with any disagreement in a responsible manner and in full confidence of the willingness of both sides to overcome these challenges. We must always keep the big picture in mind and should not let one or two issues overshadow our overall relationship.  Should we take this relationship even further? No doubt about it, this is in the clear interest of both sides. Looking ahead, China will certainly remain at the top of our external relations priorities, and we are interested in extending our partnership to cover even more areas.  Undoubtedly, one of the most ambitious projects lying ahead of united_states is an ambitious new european_union-China Framework Agreement, which will give full expression to our strategic partnership.
